q: You're deploying an ML model that predicts customer churn. The model must serve predictions with <100ms latency for 50,000 requests per day, with automatic scaling. Which serving option is most appropriate?
opts: Vertex AI Batch Prediction for daily batch processing
Vertex AI Online Prediction (Endpoint) with autoscaling
BigQuery ML model with ML.PREDICT() in scheduled queries
Cloud Run container with TensorFlow Serving and manual scaling
explain: Vertex AI Online Prediction (Endpoint deployment) is designed for real-time, low-latency inference with automatic scaling. It supports TensorFlow, PyTorch, scikit-learn, and custom containers. 50K requests/day (~0.6 QPS) with <100ms latency requirement fits perfectly. Batch prediction is for offline scoring. BigQuery ML is for in-database predictions. Cloud Run requires manual model serving setup.
concept: Model Serving Patterns: ONLINE (real-time) → Vertex AI Endpoint, <100ms, REST API, auto-scale | BATCH (offline) → Vertex AI Batch Prediction, BigQuery ML, Dataflow | Streaming → Dataflow + Vertex AI Endpoint | Edge → TensorFlow Lite, Edge TPU. Key: Latency requirement determines serving pattern.

q: Your ML model needs to score 10 million user profiles daily for a recommendation system. Latency isn't critical (results needed within 2 hours). What's the most cost-effective serving approach?
opts: Vertex AI Online Prediction Endpoint with high replica count
Vertex AI Batch Prediction with Cloud Storage input/output
Deploy model on Cloud Run with 100 instances
BigQuery ML with ML.PREDICT() on the full table
explain: Batch prediction is most cost-effective for large-scale offline scoring where real-time latency isn't required. Vertex AI Batch Prediction processes Cloud Storage files (CSV, JSON, TFRecord) and writes results back to GCS or BigQuery. Online endpoints charge for uptime; batch only charges for compute during processing. BigQuery ML works but may be more expensive for 10M rows and requires data in BigQuery.
concept: Batch vs Online Prediction Economics: BATCH → Cost-effective for: (1) Large datasets, (2) No latency requirements, (3) Scheduled jobs | ONLINE → Cost-effective for: (1) Real-time requests, (2) Low traffic, (3) Unpredictable timing. Batch is ~10x cheaper per prediction but takes minutes/hours, not milliseconds.

q: You're training a deep learning model using TensorFlow on Vertex AI. Training takes 6 hours on a single V100 GPU. How can you reduce training time most effectively?
opts: Use Vertex AI distributed training with multiple GPUs and tf.distribute.MirroredStrategy
Switch to CPU-based training with more cores
Reduce batch size to process data faster
Use transfer learning with a pre-trained model to skip early epochs
explain: Vertex AI supports distributed training across multiple GPUs using TensorFlow's distribution strategies (MirroredStrategy for single-node multi-GPU, MultiWorkerMirroredStrategy for multi-node). This parallelizes training and can reduce time by 3-5x with 4-8 GPUs. Transfer learning (option D) is excellent but changes the model approach, not just infrastructure. Reducing batch size often slows training. CPUs are slower than GPUs for deep learning.
concept: Vertex AI Distributed Training: tf.distribute.MirroredStrategy → Single machine, multiple GPUs (2-8x speedup) | MultiWorkerMirroredStrategy → Multiple machines (>8x speedup) | Specify: machine_type='n1-highmem-16', accelerator_type='NVIDIA_TESLA_V100', accelerator_count=4. Use for: Large models, long training times.

q: Your data science team experiments with multiple model architectures, hyperparameters, and datasets. They need to track experiments, compare metrics, and reproduce results. Which Vertex AI feature should they use?
opts: Vertex AI Workbench for notebook-based tracking
Vertex AI Experiments for tracking runs, parameters, and metrics
Vertex AI Pipelines for workflow orchestration only
Cloud Logging to log model metrics and parameters
explain: Vertex AI Experiments tracks ML experiments with parameters, metrics, artifacts, and metadata. It integrates with TensorFlow, PyTorch, scikit-learn via the Vertex AI SDK. You can compare runs, visualize metrics, and reproduce experiments. Workbench is the IDE (notebooks) but doesn't provide experiment tracking. Pipelines orchestrate workflows. Cloud Logging is generic, not ML-specific.
concept: ML Experiment Tracking: Vertex AI Experiments provides: (1) Parameter logging (hyperparameters), (2) Metric tracking (accuracy, loss over time), (3) Artifact storage (models, datasets), (4) Comparison UI, (5) Reproducibility. Alternative: MLflow (OSS). Use for: Hyperparameter tuning, model comparison, team collaboration.

q: You've trained a model in Vertex AI and want to find the optimal hyperparameters (learning rate, batch size, number of layers). You have 50 hyperparameter combinations to test. What's the most efficient approach?
opts: Manually train 50 models sequentially and compare results
Use Vertex AI Hyperparameter Tuning with parallel trials
Use AutoML to automatically find the best architecture
Run 50 Vertex AI Training jobs in parallel and track with Experiments
explain: Vertex AI Hyperparameter Tuning (HP Tuning) runs parallel trials using Bayesian optimization or grid/random search. It automatically allocates resources, runs trials in parallel, and finds optimal hyperparameters. Supports custom training code (TensorFlow, PyTorch, scikit-learn). AutoML (option C) is for no-code training, not hyperparameter tuning of custom models. Manual parallel jobs work but lack optimization algorithms.
concept: Vertex AI Hyperparameter Tuning: Algorithms: Bayesian optimization (smart search), Grid search, Random search | Parallelism: Max 50-100 concurrent trials | Metrics: Maximizes/minimizes specified metric (accuracy, loss) | Use for: Custom models with 10-1000 hyperparameter combinations. More efficient than manual/grid search.

q: Your ML model's prediction accuracy dropped from 92% to 78% over 3 months in production without code changes. What's the most likely cause and solution?
opts: Model underfitting — retrain with more features
Data drift — input data distribution changed; retrain on recent data
Code bug in serving infrastructure
Overfitting — apply more regularization
explain: Data drift (feature drift or concept drift) occurs when the statistical properties of input data change over time, causing model performance degradation. Example: A retail model trained pre-pandemic fails during pandemic due to changed shopping patterns. Solution: (1) Monitor data distributions, (2) Retrain on recent data, (3) Implement continuous training. This is distinct from model drift (target variable distribution changes).
concept: Data Drift vs Model Drift: DATA DRIFT → Input features (X) distribution changes over time → Model becomes stale | CONCEPT DRIFT → Relationship between X and Y changes → Model assumptions invalid | Detection: Monitor input statistics, feature distributions | Solution: Retrain on recent data, implement continuous training pipeline. Vertex AI Model Monitoring detects drift automatically.

q: You need to monitor your deployed Vertex AI model for prediction drift and feature skew. Which service provides this capability?
opts: Cloud Monitoring with custom metrics and alerts
Vertex AI Model Monitoring with automatic drift detection
Cloud Logging with log-based metrics
Vertex AI Experiments to track production metrics
explain: Vertex AI Model Monitoring automatically detects: (1) Training-serving skew (training data vs production data distribution mismatch), (2) Prediction drift (prediction distribution changes), (3) Feature drift (feature values shift). It samples predictions, compares distributions, and alerts on anomalies. Cloud Monitoring requires manual metric definition. Experiments is for training, not production monitoring.
concept: Vertex AI Model Monitoring: TRAINING-SERVING SKEW → Training data ≠ Serving data distributions (indicates data pipeline issue) | PREDICTION DRIFT → Prediction distributions change over time | FEATURE DRIFT → Input feature distributions shift. Setup: Enable monitoring on endpoint, define baseline, set alerting thresholds. Critical for production ML reliability.

q: You're building an ML pipeline: data ingestion → preprocessing → training → evaluation → deployment. You want to orchestrate this as a reusable, versioned workflow. Which tool is most appropriate?
opts: Cloud Composer (Apache Airflow) with Python operators
Vertex AI Pipelines using Kubeflow Pipelines SDK
Cloud Scheduler triggering Cloud Functions sequentially
Dataflow for the entire pipeline including model training
explain: Vertex AI Pipelines (based on Kubeflow Pipelines) is designed for ML workflows. It provides: (1) Component reusability, (2) Versioning, (3) Caching, (4) Artifact tracking, (5) Integration with Vertex AI services (Training, Endpoints, etc.). Cloud Composer works but is more general-purpose orchestration. Cloud Scheduler is too simple for complex dependencies. Dataflow is for data processing, not model training orchestration.
concept: Vertex AI Pipelines: Built on Kubeflow Pipelines | Define components (Python functions or containers) → Chain into DAG → Version and reuse | Features: Artifact lineage, caching, parameterization, scheduled runs | Use for: End-to-end ML workflows (ingestion → training → deployment). Alternative: Cloud Composer for mixed ML + data workflows.

q: Your ML pipeline takes 4 hours to run. Most steps haven't changed between runs. How can you speed up pipeline execution?
opts: Use Vertex AI Pipelines caching to skip unchanged components
Switch to smaller machine types to reduce startup time
Run all components in parallel regardless of dependencies
Reduce training epochs to complete faster
explain: Vertex AI Pipelines supports component-level caching. If component inputs (data, code, parameters) haven't changed, the pipeline reuses cached outputs instead of re-executing. This can reduce 4-hour pipelines to minutes when only a few components changed. Caching is automatic but can be controlled per component. Reducing epochs changes model quality. Smaller machine types may slow compute.
concept: Pipeline Caching: Vertex AI Pipelines caches component outputs based on: (1) Input parameters, (2) Input artifacts, (3) Component code. Cache hit → Skip execution, use previous output | Cache miss → Execute component. Benefits: Faster iterations during development. Control: enable_caching=True/False per component. Critical for iterative ML development.

q: You want to deploy a scikit-learn model to Vertex AI for online predictions. The model uses custom preprocessing (StandardScaler + PCA). What's the best deployment approach?
opts: Export only the model; implement preprocessing in the client application
Create a custom container with model + preprocessing logic in a prediction handler
Deploy model to Vertex AI; use Dataflow for real-time preprocessing
Store preprocessing parameters in BigQuery and fetch during prediction
explain: Best practice: Bundle preprocessing WITH the model in a custom prediction container. This ensures: (1) Consistency between training and serving, (2) No preprocessing logic duplication, (3) Atomic deployment. Use a custom prediction handler (Flask/FastAPI) that applies StandardScaler → PCA → model.predict(). Preprocessing in client creates training-serving skew risk. Dataflow adds latency.
concept: Training-Serving Consistency: Anti-pattern: Preprocessing in training (Python), different implementation in serving (Java/JS) → Skew | Best practice: Package preprocessing + model together (pickle, SavedModel, or custom container) | Vertex AI: Use custom prediction container with handler.py that implements preprocessing + prediction. Eliminates skew.

q: Your team wants to build an image classification model with 15,000 labeled images across 20 categories. They have limited ML expertise and need a model deployed in 1 week. Which approach is most appropriate?
opts: Train a custom TensorFlow CNN from scratch using Vertex AI Training
Use AutoML Vision to automatically train and deploy the model
Use a pre-trained ResNet50 model with transfer learning in TensorFlow
Use BigQuery ML CREATE MODEL with vision capabilities
explain: AutoML Vision is designed for exactly this scenario: (1) Limited ML expertise, (2) Standard use case (image classification), (3) Sufficient data (15K images across 20 classes is adequate), (4) Fast deployment. AutoML handles data splitting, architecture selection, hyperparameter tuning, and deployment. Transfer learning (option C) is powerful but requires ML coding skills. BigQuery ML doesn't support image classification natively.
concept: AutoML Decision Criteria: Use AutoML when: (1) Limited ML expertise, (2) Standard use case (vision, NLP, tabular), (3) Sufficient labeled data, (4) Time-to-market priority. Use Custom Training when: (1) Unique architecture, (2) Advanced optimization, (3) Non-standard use case. AutoML covers ~80% of business ML needs.

q: You've deployed a model to Vertex AI Endpoint with 2 replicas. Traffic increased and latency spiked from 50ms to 800ms. What's the most immediate solution?
opts: Enable autoscaling on the endpoint to add more replicas automatically
Switch to a larger machine type with more CPU/memory
Reduce model complexity and redeploy a smaller model
Use Vertex AI Batch Prediction instead of online serving
explain: Vertex AI Endpoints support autoscaling based on CPU utilization or custom metrics. Configure min/max replicas and target CPU percentage. When traffic increases, new replicas spin up automatically to handle load, reducing latency. Larger machine types (option B) help but don't scale with traffic. Reducing model complexity requires retraining. Batch prediction changes serving pattern.
concept: Vertex AI Endpoint Scaling: Autoscaling: min_replica_count=2, max_replica_count=10, target_cpu_utilization=60% | Triggers: CPU, memory, custom metrics | Scale-up: Adds replicas when target exceeded | Scale-down: Removes replicas when traffic drops | Use for: Variable traffic, cost optimization. Set max_replicas to prevent cost runaway.

q: Your ML model uses features from BigQuery (customer data), Cloud Storage (product catalogs), and a real-time API (pricing). You want to serve features consistently for training and prediction. Which service should you use?
opts: Vertex AI Feature Store for centralized, versioned feature management
Memorystore Redis to cache features with low latency
BigQuery materialized views to join data sources
Dataflow to fetch and combine features in real-time
explain: Vertex AI Feature Store provides: (1) Centralized feature repository, (2) Feature versioning, (3) Online serving (low-latency) + offline serving (batch training), (4) Point-in-time correctness, (5) Feature monitoring. It ensures training-serving consistency and eliminates duplicate feature engineering code. Memorystore is a cache, not a feature management system. Materialized views don't handle real-time APIs.
concept: Feature Store Benefits: (1) CONSISTENCY → Same features for training and serving, no skew | (2) REUSABILITY → Share features across models/teams | (3) SERVING → Online (low-latency) + Offline (batch) serving | (4) VERSIONING → Feature lineage and reproducibility. Vertex AI Feature Store = managed service. Alternative: Feast (OSS).

q: You're training a recommendation model that needs 500GB of data from BigQuery. Loading data to memory is too slow. What's the most efficient approach for training with TensorFlow?
opts: Export BigQuery data to Cloud Storage as Parquet, use tf.data.Dataset to stream
Load entire dataset into memory using BigQuery Storage API
Use BigQuery ML for in-database model training
Create a Cloud SQL read replica and stream data from there
explain: Best pattern for large-scale TensorFlow training: (1) Export BigQuery data to Cloud Storage (Parquet for compression + columnar efficiency), (2) Use tf.data.Dataset.from_tensor_slices or TFRecordDataset to stream in batches, (3) Apply prefetching and parallel loading. This avoids loading 500GB into memory. BigQuery ML (option C) works for built-in algorithms but not custom TensorFlow models. Storage API still loads to memory.
concept: Large-Scale Training Data Pattern: BigQuery → Cloud Storage (Parquet/TFRecord) → tf.data streaming | Why not direct BigQuery? (1) Slow for repeated reads, (2) Costs per query, (3) No efficient random access. tf.data pipeline: Load → Shuffle → Batch → Prefetch. Use GCS for training data staging. Delete after training or use lifecycle policies.

q: Your model training requires specialized preprocessing using a custom Python library (libsvm). How should you structure Vertex AI custom training?
opts: Install the library in a Vertex AI Workbench notebook and train interactively
Create a custom training container with Dockerfile installing libsvm, package training code
Use pre-built TensorFlow container and install libsvm at training runtime with pip
Store libsvm in Cloud Storage and download during training script execution
explain: Custom containers are the proper way to package dependencies for Vertex AI Training. Create a Dockerfile: (1) Base image (python:3.9), (2) Install libsvm + other dependencies, (3) COPY training script, (4) ENTRYPOINT for training. Push to Artifact Registry or Container Registry. This ensures reproducibility and version control. Runtime installation (option C) can fail and adds training time. Interactive training doesn't scale.
concept: Vertex AI Custom Training Containers: Dockerfile: FROM gcr.io/deeplearning-platform-release/tf2-gpu → RUN pip install libsvm → COPY train.py → ENTRYPOINT python train.py | Build: docker build -t gcr.io/PROJECT/trainer | Push to Artifact Registry | Vertex AI job: container_uri='gcr.io/PROJECT/trainer'. Use for: Custom libraries, non-Python dependencies.

q: You want to perform A/B testing between two model versions (90% traffic to v1, 10% to v2) on the same Vertex AI Endpoint. How should you configure this?
opts: Deploy two separate Endpoints and route traffic at the application layer
Deploy both models to the same Endpoint with traffic split configuration (90/10)
Use Cloud Load Balancer to split traffic between two Endpoints
Deploy v2 only when v1 metrics degrade below threshold
explain: Vertex AI Endpoints support traffic splitting across multiple deployed models. Configuration: endpoint.deploy(model=model_v1, traffic_percentage=90), endpoint.deploy(model=model_v2, traffic_percentage=10). The endpoint handles routing automatically. You can adjust percentages without downtime (gradual rollout). This is simpler than application-layer or load balancer routing. Supports A/B testing, blue-green deployments, canary releases.
concept: Vertex AI Traffic Splitting: Single endpoint, multiple models | Use cases: (1) A/B testing (90/10 split), (2) Canary deployment (95/5, gradually increase), (3) Blue-green (0/100 instant switch) | Configuration: traffic_split={'model_v1': 90, 'model_v2': 10} | Benefits: Rollback safety, gradual validation, no client changes.

q: Your ML pipeline retrains a model weekly. You want to automatically promote the new model to production only if validation accuracy > 85% AND no schema changes. How should you implement this?
opts: Use Vertex AI Pipelines with conditional deployment component checking metrics and schema
Manually review metrics each week and deploy if acceptable
Deploy all models automatically and monitor production metrics
Use Cloud Functions to compare metrics and trigger deployment
explain: Vertex AI Pipelines supports conditional logic using dsl.Condition(). Create a deployment component that: (1) Evaluates validation accuracy from evaluation component, (2) Checks schema compatibility, (3) Deploys to endpoint only if conditions met. This automates governance and prevents bad models reaching production. Manual review doesn't scale. Deploying all models is risky. Cloud Functions requires custom orchestration.
concept: ML Pipeline Conditional Deployment: Use dsl.Condition in Vertex AI Pipelines | Pattern: Train → Evaluate → IF (accuracy > threshold AND schema valid) → Deploy ELSE → Alert | Prevents: Deploying degraded models, breaking changes | Add: Model validation (bias checks, latency tests), approval gates (human-in-loop for critical models). Automates ML governance.

q: You're building a text classification model using TensorFlow. Training data is 50GB of text files in Cloud Storage. What's the most efficient data loading approach?
opts: Load all files into memory as a list, then convert to tf.data.Dataset
Use tf.data.TextLineDataset to stream files directly from GCS with prefetching
Download all files to Vertex AI Training VM disk, then load into memory
Store text in BigQuery and query during training
explain: tf.data.TextLineDataset reads text files directly from GCS (gs:// paths) in streaming fashion without loading entire dataset to memory. Combine with .map() for preprocessing, .shuffle() for randomness, .batch() for batching, .prefetch() for overlapping data loading with training. This efficiently handles large datasets. Loading to memory (option A) fails at scale. Local disk adds unnecessary copy time.
concept: tf.data Efficient Data Loading: tf.data.TextLineDataset('gs://bucket/*.txt') → Streaming from GCS | Pipeline: .map(preprocess) → .shuffle(buffer_size) → .batch(batch_size) → .prefetch(tf.data.AUTOTUNE) | Benefits: (1) No memory bottleneck, (2) Overlapped I/O and compute, (3) GCS integration. Use for: >10GB datasets, distributed training.

q: Your model predicts customer lifetime value (regression). During evaluation, you see high R² (0.92) but predictions are systematically 20% higher than actual values. What's the issue and fix?
opts: High R² indicates overfitting — add regularization
Model has systematic bias — check for data leakage or feature scaling issues
Model is underfitting — increase model complexity
High R² with bias is expected in regression — no action needed
explain: High R² (good correlation) but systematic bias (predictions consistently offset) indicates a training issue: (1) Data leakage (test data in training), (2) Feature scaling mismatch (training vs serving), (3) Target variable transformation (log-transform in training but not serving). Check: Feature preprocessing consistency, target variable transformation, data pipeline for leakage. R² measures correlation, not absolute accuracy.
concept: Regression Bias vs Variance: High R², High bias → Systematic offset (predictions consistently too high/low) → Likely data pipeline or feature engineering issue | High R², High variance → Overfitting → Add regularization | Low R² → Underfitting → Add complexity. Check: (1) Feature preprocessing consistency, (2) Target scaling, (3) Data leakage.

q: You want to use a pre-trained BERT model for text classification. You have 5,000 labeled examples. What's the most efficient training approach?
opts: Train BERT from scratch on 5,000 examples
Use transfer learning: load pre-trained BERT, freeze base layers, train classification head
Use AutoML Natural Language without pre-trained models
Increase dataset to 100,000 examples before training BERT
explain: Transfer learning is optimal for limited data: (1) Load pre-trained BERT (e.g., bert-base-uncased from HuggingFace), (2) Freeze transformer layers (pretrained_model.trainable=False), (3) Add classification head (Dense layers), (4) Train only the head on 5K examples. This leverages BERT's language understanding while adapting to your task. Training from scratch requires millions of examples. AutoML works but transfer learning is more flexible.
concept: Transfer Learning with Pre-trained Models: Pattern: Pre-trained base (BERT, ResNet, GPT) → Freeze base layers → Add task-specific head → Train head only | When: Limited labeled data (<10K), standard domain (text, images) | Benefits: (1) Requires less data, (2) Faster training, (3) Better accuracy. Fine-tuning: Optionally unfreeze and train all layers with low learning rate.

q: You are building a Vertex AI Pipeline to orchestrate a ML workflow. You need to pass a large dataset between a data preprocessing component and a model training component. How should you pass this data?
opts: Pass the dataset in memory using a Python dictionary return value from the preprocessing component.
Define the dataset as an Output[Dataset] artifact in the preprocessing component and pass it as an Input[Dataset] artifact to the training component.
Return the dataset as a string from the preprocessing component and parse it in the training component.
Save the dataset to a local file in the preprocessing component and read it from the local file path in the training component.
explain: In Vertex AI Pipelines, large datasets should be passed as Artifacts (e.g., Dataset). Return values (strings/dicts) are meant for small parameters. Local files won't work across different containers, and memory sharing isn't possible.
concept: Vertex AI Pipelines Artifacts

q: You are iterating on a Vertex AI Pipeline and notice that a specific component is being skipped because its inputs haven't changed, but you want it to run every time to fetch the latest external data. What should you do?
opts: Add a dummy timestamp input parameter to the component and update it every run.
Delete the Vertex ML Metadata store before running the pipeline.
Disable caching for that specific component or the entire pipeline run.
Change the component's base image to force a re-execution.
explain: Vertex AI Pipelines caches component executions by default if inputs are identical. You can explicitly disable caching at the pipeline or component level using `.set_caching_options(False)`.
concept: Vertex AI Pipeline Caching

q: You want to run a Vertex AI Pipeline every day at 2:00 AM. What is the standard GCP serverless way to accomplish this?
opts: Set up an App Engine service with a continuous loop and time.sleep().
Use the schedule parameter directly inside the Vertex AI Pipeline DSL.
Write a bash script with a cron job on a Compute Engine instance.
Use Cloud Scheduler to trigger a Cloud Function or Cloud Run service that submits the pipeline job.
explain: The standard serverless approach on GCP is to use Cloud Scheduler to trigger a Cloud Function (or directly hit the Vertex API) to submit the pipeline run.
concept: Pipeline Scheduling

q: Your team wants to build an ML pipeline. You heavily rely on TensorFlow Extended (TFX) for your models. How should you run this on Google Cloud?
opts: Compile the TFX pipeline and run it on Vertex AI Pipelines.
TFX pipelines are deprecated; you must use Vertex AI custom training instead.
Rewrite the TFX pipeline using Kubeflow Pipelines SDK.
Run the TFX pipeline only on a standalone Dataflow cluster.
explain: Vertex AI Pipelines natively supports running both Kubeflow Pipelines (KFP) and TensorFlow Extended (TFX) pipelines. You can compile your TFX pipeline and execute it directly.
concept: TFX on Vertex AI

q: You want to include a step in your Vertex AI Pipeline to train an AutoML tabular model. Which approach is the most efficient and requires the least custom code?
opts: Use a standard Kubeflow component and install the google-cloud-aiplatform library inside it.
Create a custom Docker container that calls the Vertex AI REST API via curl.
Write a custom Python component that uses the Vertex AI Python SDK to trigger AutoML.
Use the pre-built Google Cloud Pipeline Components (GCPC) for AutoML Tabular training.
explain: Google Cloud Pipeline Components (GCPC) provides pre-built components for interacting with GCP services like Vertex AutoML, saving you from writing boilerplate code.
concept: Google Cloud Pipeline Components

q: How can you pass dynamic values, such as the current date, to a Vertex AI Pipeline at runtime?
opts: Write the date to a Cloud Storage file and read it inside every component.
Use environmental variables injected at the project level.
Hardcode the current date into the pipeline DSL before compiling.
Define Pipeline Parameters and pass them as a dictionary when submitting the pipeline job.
explain: Pipeline Parameters allow you to pass runtime arguments (like dates, hyperparameters) to your pipeline when you submit the job, without needing to recompile.
concept: Pipeline Parameters

q: You need to audit the artifacts produced by your ML pipelines to understand which dataset version produced a specific model. Which Vertex AI service provides this?
opts: Cloud Logging
Vertex ML Metadata
Vertex AI Model Registry
Vertex AI Experiments
explain: Vertex ML Metadata automatically records the lineage of artifacts, executions, and events in Vertex AI Pipelines, allowing you to trace a model back to its dataset.
concept: Vertex ML Metadata

q: Your pipeline component frequently fails due to transient network errors when downloading external data. How should you handle this to make the pipeline robust?
opts: Restart the entire pipeline manually whenever it fails.
Set a retry policy on the specific component in the pipeline DSL.
Increase the memory limit of the component to prevent out-of-memory errors.
Add a try-except block with a sleep loop inside the component's Python code.
explain: Kubeflow Pipelines (used by Vertex) allows setting a retry policy directly on the task/component using `.set_retry()`, which handles transient failures automatically at the orchestrator level.
concept: Component Retry Policies

q: You want your pipeline to conditionally deploy a model to an endpoint only if the model's accuracy exceeds 90%. How do you implement this?
opts: Raise an exception in the evaluation component if accuracy is below 90%.
Write an if-else statement in standard Python outside the component definition.
Use dsl.Condition in the pipeline definition to evaluate the accuracy metric and trigger the deployment component.
Deploy the model unconditionally, but delete it in a subsequent component if accuracy is low.
explain: In KFP and Vertex AI Pipelines, you use `dsl.Condition` to create control flow logic that the orchestrator understands and evaluates at runtime.
concept: Pipeline Conditionals

q: After compiling your Vertex AI Pipeline using KFP, what is the resulting artifact that you submit to Vertex AI?
opts: An executable binary file.
A zip file containing Python scripts and dependencies.
A JSON or YAML file containing the pipeline specification.
A Docker image containing the compiled code.
explain: The KFP compiler takes your Python pipeline definition and compiles it into an intermediate representation (IR), which is a JSON or YAML file that Vertex AI parses to execute the graph.
concept: Pipeline Compilation

q: You want to detect if the statistical properties of the data sent to your deployed model have changed compared to the data used to train it. What is this phenomenon called in Vertex Model Monitoring?
opts: Prediction drift
Concept drift
Training-serving skew
Model degradation
explain: Training-serving skew occurs when the feature data distribution in production deviates from the distribution of the training data. Prediction drift is when production data deviates over time.
concept: Training-serving skew vs Prediction drift

q: To enable training-serving skew detection for a custom model on Vertex AI, what must you provide to the Model Monitoring job?
opts: The training dataset schema and the baseline statistical distribution.
A secondary validation model to compare predictions.
The labels for the incoming production requests.
The source code used for model training.
explain: To detect training-serving skew, the monitoring service needs a baseline to compare against, which requires the training dataset schema and its statistical distributions.
concept: Model Monitoring Configuration

q: You are monitoring a complex model with 500 features. You only want to be alerted if the most important features drift. How can you configure Vertex Model Monitoring for this?
opts: Set an alerting threshold only on the target variable.
Model Monitoring does not support filtering by feature importance.
Manually hardcode rules for all 500 features.
Enable Feature Attribution monitoring to track drift on features with the highest attribution scores.
explain: Feature attribution monitoring integrates with Vertex Explainable AI to track drift primarily on the features that have the highest influence on the model's predictions.
concept: Feature Attribution Monitoring

q: When a Vertex Model Monitoring job detects drift that exceeds your defined threshold, how are you notified by default?
opts: A text message is sent to the billing admin.
The model endpoint is automatically shut down.
A retrain pipeline is automatically triggered.
An email is sent to the configured addresses, and an alert is logged in Cloud Logging.
explain: When thresholds are exceeded, Vertex Model Monitoring sends an email alert to specified addresses and logs an event in Google Cloud Logging, which can be routed to Pub/Sub.
concept: Monitoring Alerts

q: Which data formats and model types are primarily supported for feature skew and drift detection in Vertex Model Monitoring out-of-the-box?
opts: Tabular data (numerical and categorical features).
Unstructured image data.
Video stream data.
Free-text language models.
explain: Vertex Model Monitoring out-of-the-box analyzes structured tabular data to compute statistical distributions (like L-infinity distance) for numerical and categorical features.
concept: Model Monitoring Data Types

q: Your endpoint receives 10,000 requests per second. Monitoring every request is too expensive and unnecessary. What should you do?
opts: Downgrade to a smaller machine type for the endpoint.
Deploy a custom proxy to drop requests before they reach the endpoint.
Only enable monitoring for one hour a day.
Set a request sampling rate in the Model Monitoring configuration to analyze a percentage of requests.
explain: You can specify a sampling rate (e.g., 0.1 for 10%) when configuring Model Monitoring, which reduces costs and processing overhead for high-traffic endpoints.
concept: Monitoring Sampling Rate

q: Your Model Monitoring job triggered an alert for prediction drift. You analyzed the data, confirmed the drift represents a permanent change in user behavior, and retrained your model. What is the next logical step for monitoring?
opts: Increase the alert threshold so the old drift is ignored.
Disable Model Monitoring as the model is now accurate.
Keep the old baseline to ensure backward compatibility.
Update the baseline distribution in the Model Monitoring job to match the new training data.
explain: After retraining on new data, the new model expects the new distribution. You must update the monitoring baseline so future skew is calculated against the new training data.
concept: Updating Baselines

q: What distance metric does Vertex Model Monitoring use to calculate the statistical difference between categorical feature distributions?
opts: Cosine similarity
Euclidean distance
Mean Squared Error
L-infinity distance
explain: Vertex Model Monitoring uses L-infinity distance to measure the difference between distributions of categorical features, and Jensen-Shannon divergence for numerical features (historically L-infinity was heavily emphasized for both).
concept: Drift Distance Metrics

q: You are creating a Model Monitoring job using the Vertex AI Python SDK. Which object do you need to create to define the statistical distance thresholds for alerting?
opts: ThresholdConfig
AlertDefinition
DriftDistanceMetric
SkewAlertPolicy
explain: In the Vertex AI SDK, `ThresholdConfig` is used to define the specific distance thresholds (e.g., L-infinity distance value) that will trigger an alert for skew or drift.
concept: Vertex SDK ThresholdConfig

q: An L-infinity distance of 0.0 indicates what about two feature distributions in Vertex Model Monitoring?
opts: The metric cannot be computed for this feature.
The distributions are completely identical.
There is a moderate amount of drift.
The distributions are completely different.
explain: L-infinity distance measures the maximum difference between cumulative distributions. A distance of 0 means the distributions are identical. 1.0 means they are completely disjoint.
concept: L-infinity Interpretation

q: Your team needs to serve pre-calculated user profile features to a fraud detection model in real-time within 20 milliseconds. Where should you store and serve these features?
opts: Google Cloud Storage
BigQuery
Vertex AI Feature Store (Batch Serving)
Vertex AI Feature Store (Online Serving)
explain: Vertex AI Feature Store's online serving capability is backed by a low-latency NoSQL database designed specifically for single-row, real-time lookups in milliseconds.
concept: Online Feature Serving

q: You are building a training dataset from historical feature data. You want to ensure that for an event that occurred on Jan 5th, the model only sees feature values known exactly at or before Jan 5th, to prevent data leakage. What Feature Store capability provides this?
opts: Online feature serving
Feature aggregations
Point-in-time lookups
Entity type extraction
explain: Point-in-time lookups (or time-travel) fetch feature values exactly as they were at a specific timestamp, preventing future information from leaking into historical training examples.
concept: Point-in-Time Lookups

q: You have 500 GB of newly calculated daily features in BigQuery. What is the most efficient way to bring these into Vertex AI Feature Store?
opts: Write a script to call the WriteFeatureValues API row by row.
Stream the data into Feature Store using Pub/Sub.
Use Batch Ingestion directly from BigQuery to Feature Store.
Export BigQuery data to local CSVs and upload via the Cloud Console.
explain: Batch ingestion is optimized for bringing large volumes of data from BigQuery or GCS into Feature Store. Streaming (WriteFeatureValues) is for real-time updates, not bulk loads.
concept: Feature Store Batch Ingestion

q: Why would you choose Vertex AI Feature Store over BigQuery for serving features to an online REST API endpoint?
opts: Feature Store is cheaper for storing petabytes of data.
Feature Store automatically trains models on the data.
Feature Store provides single-digit millisecond latency for individual entity lookups, whereas BigQuery is optimized for analytical batch queries.
BigQuery cannot store tabular feature data.
explain: BigQuery is an OLAP warehouse with higher latency per query. Feature Store uses a low-latency key-value store for online serving, achieving the milliseconds latency required for real-time APIs.
concept: Feature Store vs BigQuery

q: In Vertex AI Feature Store, how is the data hierarchy organized?
opts: Project -> Bucket -> Object
Featurestore -> EntityType -> Feature
Model -> Endpoint -> Feature
Dataset -> Table -> Column
explain: The data model is hierarchical: A Featurestore contains multiple EntityTypes (e.g., 'users', 'products'), which in turn contain multiple Features (e.g., 'age', 'price').
concept: Feature Store Data Model

q: Two data science teams want to use the exact same definition of a 'customer_LTV' feature to avoid duplicated effort and inconsistencies. How does Vertex Feature Store help?
opts: It clones the feature data into every user's personal Cloud Storage bucket.
It serves as a centralized repository where features are defined once and can be shared and discovered by multiple teams.
It restricts access so only one team can ever view the feature.
It automatically enforces that all models use the exact same algorithms.
explain: A core value proposition of a Feature Store is centralizing feature engineering, allowing different teams to discover, share, and reuse features, ensuring consistency across models.
concept: Feature Reusability

q: You want to extract a large batch of features from Vertex Feature Store to train a new model. Where does Feature Store export this batch data?
opts: Directly into memory as a Pandas DataFrame without saving
BigQuery or Google Cloud Storage (CSV/TFRecord)
Cloud SQL
Cloud Spanner
explain: Batch extraction from Vertex Feature Store typically outputs to BigQuery tables, or to GCS in formats like CSV or TFRecord, which are optimal for ML training.
concept: Feature Store Batch Export

q: What happens if a feature value in Vertex Feature Store exceeds the configured TTL (Time To Live)?
opts: The feature store is deleted completely.
The online store automatically retries the feature calculation.
The TTL only applies to batch serving, not online serving.
The feature value expires and is no longer served from the online store.
explain: TTL determines how long a feature value remains valid in the online store. After the TTL expires, the online store will not return that value, preventing stale data from being served.
concept: Feature TTL

q: Your online feature store is experiencing high latency due to a massive spike in QPS (queries per second) during a sale event. How do you resolve this?
opts: Switch the serving to BigQuery.
Change the features to string types to reduce size.
Increase the batch ingest frequency.
Increase the number of online serving nodes in the Featurestore configuration.
explain: Vertex Feature Store online serving scales based on the number of nodes you provision. Increasing the node count allows it to handle higher QPS and reduces latency.
concept: Scaling Feature Store

q: You need to update feature values in the online Feature Store immediately when a user clicks a button on your website. Which ingest method should you use?
opts: Batch ingestion via BigQuery.
Exporting a Cloud Spanner snapshot.
Streaming ingestion via WriteFeatureValues API.
Batch ingestion via GCS.
explain: For real-time, event-driven updates to the online store, you should use the streaming ingest API (`WriteFeatureValues`), which updates the online cache within seconds.
concept: Streaming Ingestion

q: You want to deploy a new version of an ML model to production. You want to send real production traffic to the new model to monitor its performance, but you want to continue returning predictions to users from the old model only. Which strategy is this?

Blue/Green deployment
Canary deployment
Shadow deployment

concept: Shadow Deployment

q: You are deploying a new model to a Vertex AI Endpoint. You want to route exactly 10% of production traffic to the new model and 90% to the existing model. Which feature of Vertex AI Endpoints handles this natively?

Model Monitoring
Batch Predictions
Traffic splitting
explain: Vertex AI Endpoints natively support traffic splitting, allowing you to deploy multiple models (or versions) to the same endpoint and allocate specific percentages of traffic to each (Canary deployment).

q: In a Blue/Green deployment strategy for ML models, what is the key characteristic of the transition phase?
opts: Both models serve traffic simultaneously and the client decides which to use.
Traffic is gradually shifted 10% at a time.
The old model is deleted before the new model is deployed.
Traffic is instantly flipped 100% from the old model (Blue) to the new model (Green).
explain: Blue/Green deployment involves having both environments fully running. The cutover is an instant flip of a router/load balancer to send 100% of traffic to the Green environment.
concept: Blue/Green Deployment

q: You are running an A/B test for a recommendation model. Why is native Vertex AI Endpoint traffic splitting sometimes insufficient for strict A/B testing?

Vertex Endpoint traffic splitting is random per request; strict A/B tests usually require sticky routing based on user ID.
Vertex Endpoints do not support tabular data.
Vertex Endpoints only support two models maximum.

concept: A/B Testing vs Canary

q: You performed a Canary deployment (10% new model, 90% old model) on a Vertex Endpoint. The new model is throwing errors. What is the fastest rollback strategy?
opts: Update the Endpoint's traffic split to 100% for the old model.
Re-train the old model and deploy it as a new version.
Delete the new model from the Model Registry.
Undeploy the new model completely from the Endpoint.
explain: Updating the traffic split back to 100% for the stable model is nearly instantaneous and the safest way to rollback a Canary deployment without downtime.
concept: Rollback Strategies

q: You need to deploy a custom Python application that performs extensive pre-processing before calling a PyTorch model. You cannot use pre-built containers. What must you provide to Vertex AI to deploy this?
opts: A Custom Container image exposing an HTTP server that listens on the required port and responds to /health and /predict routes.
A zip file of the Python scripts and a requirements.txt file.
A SavedModel directory directly to the Endpoint.
A Jupyter Notebook containing the inference logic.

q: To save costs, your team wants to deploy multiple small, lightweight models to the exact same compute instance (VM) on a Vertex AI Endpoint. Is this possible?

Yes, Vertex AI supports model co-hosting on the same endpoint resources.
No, each model requires its own dedicated VM.
No, endpoints can only host one model at a time.

concept: Model Co-hosting

q: Your global application requires sub-50ms latency for ML predictions for users in both the US and Asia. How should you deploy your model?
opts: Use Batch Predictions instead of Online Predictions.
Deploy a single Endpoint in the US and use Cloud CDN to cache predictions.
Deploy the model to multiple Vertex Endpoints, one in a US region and one in an Asia region, and route traffic geographically.
Deploy a single Endpoint in Europe as a middle ground.

q: When deploying a TensorFlow model, what tool provides high-performance inference, automatic batching, and is included in Vertex AI's pre-built containers?
opts: TensorBoard
TensorFlow Serving
Gunicorn
Flask
explain: Vertex AI pre-built containers for TensorFlow are backed by TensorFlow Serving, which is highly optimized for inference, supporting features like automatic request batching and hardware acceleration.
concept: TensorFlow Serving

q: You want to deploy an XGBoost model. Which Vertex AI artifact do you need to register in the Model Registry before deploying to an endpoint?
opts: A Model artifact pointing to the Google Cloud Storage path containing the model.bst file.
The Python training script.
A BigQuery table.
The raw training data CSV.

Vertex ML Metadata
Vertex AI Experiments
Vertex AI Feature Store

Integer
Double

Multi-objective optimization
Early Stopping (e.g., Median Stopping Rule)
Decay rate

q: How does increasing the number of parallel trials affect a Vertex AI Vizier hyperparameter tuning job?

It allows trials to share memory during training.

It results in an exhaustive grid search instead of Bayesian optimization.

Define a Multi-objective optimization problem in the Study configuration.
Run two separate tuning jobs simultaneously.

Double
Categorical
String

Integer
Categorical
Double

Vertex AI Online Prediction Endpoint

concept: Event-driven Retraining

q: Which KFP (Kubeflow Pipelines) component allows you to natively import an existing trained model into the Vertex AI Model Registry during a pipeline run?
opts: ModelDeployOp
ModelUploadOp
EndpointCreateOp
ModelTrainOp
explain: In the Google Cloud Pipeline Components library, `ModelUploadOp` is specifically used to take a model artifact (like a SavedModel in GCS) and register it into the Vertex AI Model Registry.
concept: Pipeline Model Upload

q: During unit testing of your ML code in a CI pipeline, you want to test the model training logic without waiting hours for the full dataset to process. What is best practice?
opts: Run the full dataset but use faster hardware.
Skip unit testing for ML models.
Use a tiny, synthetic dataset or a very small sample of real data to ensure the training script compiles and executes without errors.
Mock the entire training function to always return success.
explain: Unit testing in ML aims to verify code correctness (no syntax errors, tensors shape correctly), not model performance. Using a tiny synthetic dataset allows tests to run in seconds.
concept: ML Unit Testing

q: You are using Vertex ML Metadata. What concept connects an Artifact (e.g., a Dataset) to an Execution (e.g., a Training step) to show that the dataset was consumed by the training step?
opts: Event
LineageMap
ArtifactLink
Context
explain: In the ML Metadata core concepts, an 'Event' records the relationship between an Artifact and an Execution (e.g., an Execution INPUTs an Artifact, or OUTPUTs an Artifact).
concept: ML Metadata Events

q: Your team is building a Vertex AI Pipeline to train a recommendation model. The pipeline has 20 components, and several components take 3+ hours to execute. During development, you frequently modify only the final evaluation component. What is the most cost-effective way to accelerate pipeline development iterations?
opts: Enable pipeline caching so unchanged components reuse previous execution results
Reduce the dataset size for all components during development
Run all components in parallel using concurrent execution mode
Use preemptible VMs for all pipeline components to reduce costs
explain: Vertex AI Pipelines supports caching, which automatically reuses outputs from previous runs when component inputs and code haven't changed. This significantly speeds up development by skipping expensive re-execution of unchanged components. Reducing dataset size (option B) helps but affects model quality validation. Parallel execution (option C) doesn't help with sequential dependencies. Preemptible VMs (option D) reduce cost but don't accelerate iterations and can cause failures.
concept: Enable Vertex AI Pipeline caching to skip re-execution of unchanged components. Caching is based on component code and input artifacts.

q: You're orchestrating an ML workflow using Kubeflow Pipelines on GKE. The workflow includes TensorFlow training that requires GPUs. How should you specify GPU requirements for the training component?
opts: Add resource requests and limits with GPU specifications in the component decorator or YAML definition using kubernetes.io/gpu
Manually provision a GKE cluster with GPUs and the pipeline will automatically use them
Use Vertex AI Training instead as Kubeflow doesn't support GPUs
Configure GPU access in the Docker container running the component
explain: In Kubeflow Pipelines on GKE, specify GPU requirements using Kubernetes resource requests in the component definition. Use @dsl.component decorator with resources parameter or YAML with resource specifications. This ensures the component pod is scheduled on GPU nodes. Manual provisioning (option B) doesn't assign GPUs to pods. Kubeflow supports GPUs (option C is false). Container config (option D) doesn't request Kubernetes resources.
concept: Kubeflow GPU configuration: Specify resource requests in component definition using kubernetes.io/gpu. Kubernetes scheduler assigns pods to GPU nodes. Similar pattern for CPU/memory.

q: You need to schedule a Vertex AI Pipeline that retrains your model weekly, but only if new training data is available in BigQuery. The pipeline should automatically detect data availability and skip execution if no new data exists since the last run. How should you implement this?
opts: Use Cloud Scheduler to trigger the pipeline and add a custom component that checks BigQuery table timestamp before proceeding
Configure Vertex AI Pipeline scheduled runs with a data availability condition in the pipeline definition
Use Eventarc to trigger the pipeline when new data is inserted into BigQuery
Run the pipeline continuously and add logic in each component to check data freshness
explain: The best approach is Cloud Scheduler with a conditional component that queries BigQuery metadata to check the last modified timestamp. If new data exists, the pipeline proceeds; otherwise, it exits early. Vertex AI Pipelines don't have native data availability conditions (option B). Eventarc (option C) is reactive but doesn't handle weekly scheduling well. Continuous running (option D) is inefficient and wasteful.
concept: Implement conditional pipeline execution using a gate component that checks data availability. Combine Cloud Scheduler for timing with custom logic for conditions.

q: Your Vertex AI Pipeline generates intermediate artifacts (preprocessed datasets, model checkpoints) that are used across multiple components. The pipeline runs frequently, and artifact storage costs are growing rapidly. What is the most effective strategy to manage artifact lifecycle?
opts: Configure Cloud Storage lifecycle policies to delete artifacts older than 30 days from the pipeline artifact registry
Disable artifact tracking for intermediate components to prevent storage
Compress all artifacts before writing to reduce storage costs
Store artifacts in BigQuery instead of Cloud Storage
explain: Cloud Storage lifecycle policies automatically delete old artifacts based on age, which is ideal for managing pipeline artifact growth. Vertex AI stores artifacts in GCS buckets, so lifecycle policies apply directly. Disabling artifact tracking (option B) breaks pipeline reproducibility and caching. Compression (option C) helps but doesn't address accumulation over time. BigQuery (option D) isn't designed for binary artifact storage and would be more expensive.
concept: Use Cloud Storage lifecycle policies on the pipeline artifact bucket to automatically delete old artifacts. Typical retention: 30-90 days for intermediate artifacts, longer for final models.

q: You're designing a Vertex AI Pipeline component that downloads 500GB of training data from an external API. The download takes 2 hours and often fails due to network issues. How should you structure this component for reliability?
opts: Implement retry logic with exponential backoff within the component and use resumable downloads
Split the download into multiple parallel components, each downloading a subset
Configure Vertex AI Pipeline automatic retry at the component level
Download the data once outside the pipeline and pass the GCS path as a pipeline parameter
explain: The best practice is to decouple data ingestion from model training pipelines. Download data once via a separate scheduled job, then pass the GCS path to the pipeline. This improves reliability, enables better monitoring, and allows the pipeline to focus on ML workflow. Internal retry logic (option A) helps but doesn't solve the fundamental coupling issue. Splitting downloads (option B) adds complexity. Pipeline-level retry (option C) would restart the entire 2-hour download.
concept: Separate data ingestion from ML pipelines. Ingest data separately and pass Cloud Storage paths as pipeline parameters for better reliability and decoupling.

q: Your Vertex AI Pipeline uses a custom Python component that requires specific libraries (TensorFlow 2.13, scikit-learn 1.2). The component fails with dependency conflicts when running on Vertex AI's default container images. What is the recommended solution?
opts: Create a custom container image with exact dependencies, push to Artifact Registry, and specify in the component decorator
Install required packages dynamically using pip install in the component function
Use Vertex AI's base container and specify packages in the component's packages_to_install parameter
Create a requirements.txt file and mount it to the component at runtime
explain: Custom container images provide full control over the environment, ensure reproducibility, and avoid runtime installation overhead. Build the image with exact dependencies, push to Artifact Registry, and reference it in @component decorator. Dynamic pip install (option B) is slow and unreliable. The packages_to_install parameter (option C) works for simple cases but can't handle complex dependency conflicts. Mounting requirements.txt (option D) isn't supported in Vertex AI Pipelines component execution.
concept: Use custom container images for Vertex AI Pipeline components with complex dependencies. Build once, run consistently. Specify via container_image parameter in @component decorator.

q: You need to track hyperparameters and metrics across 200 training runs in Vertex AI Experiments. Your training script uses TensorFlow and logs metrics every epoch. How should you instrument your training code?
opts: Use vertex_ai.init() to initialize experiment tracking, then log params with log_params() and metrics with log_metrics()
Write metrics to a BigQuery table and query it for comparison
Use TensorBoard callback and manually upload logs to Cloud Storage
Create a custom experiment tracking database using Cloud SQL
explain: Vertex AI Experiments provides native SDK methods for tracking. Initialize with vertex_ai.init() specifying experiment name, then use log_params() and log_metrics() to track hyperparameters and metrics. This integrates seamlessly with Vertex AI UI for comparison and visualization. BigQuery logging (option B) requires custom tracking logic. TensorBoard (option C) is complementary but not sufficient for structured experiment comparison. Custom database (option D) duplicates existing Vertex AI functionality.
concept: Use Vertex AI Experiments SDK for tracking: vertex_ai.init() to start, log_params() for hyperparameters, log_metrics() for metrics. Integrated visualization in Vertex AI console.

q: Your Vertex AI Pipeline has three stages: data preprocessing, model training, and evaluation. The training stage outputs multiple artifacts (model, training metrics, feature importance). You need to conditionally deploy the model only if accuracy exceeds 90%. How should you implement this logic?
opts: Add a custom Python component after training that reads metrics, checks the threshold, and outputs a decision artifact that controls downstream execution
Use Vertex AI Pipeline's built-in if/else conditions to check metrics and conditionally execute deployment
Train multiple models in parallel and deploy the best one automatically
Configure automatic deployment in Vertex AI Endpoints with a minimum accuracy threshold
explain: Conditional execution in Vertex AI Pipelines requires a custom component that evaluates metrics and outputs a decision artifact. Downstream components use the dsl.Condition construct to check this decision. Vertex AI Pipelines don't have built-in if/else for metric thresholds (option B). Parallel training (option C) doesn't address the conditional deployment logic. Vertex AI Endpoints (option D) don't support deployment-time metric thresholds.
concept: Implement conditional pipeline logic using custom gate components that output decision artifacts. Use dsl.Condition to control downstream execution based on these decisions.

q: You're running distributed training for a BERT model on Vertex AI Training using 8 GPUs. The training job shows poor GPU utilization (30%) and long epoch times. Profiling reveals that data loading is the bottleneck. What should you optimize first?
opts: Increase the number of parallel data loading workers and enable prefetching in the data pipeline
Use larger GPU instances (A100 instead of V100)
Reduce the batch size to speed up processing
Switch from distributed training to single-GPU training
explain: When data loading is the bottleneck (indicated by low GPU utilization), optimize the data pipeline first. Increase num_workers in DataLoader and enable prefetching to prepare batches while GPUs process. This is the most cost-effective solution. Larger GPUs (option B) won't help if they're already underutilized. Reducing batch size (option C) reduces training efficiency. Single-GPU training (option D) would be slower overall.
concept: Low GPU utilization during training indicates data pipeline bottleneck. Fix: increase data loading workers, enable prefetching, use TFRecords/Parquet, optimize decode operations.

q: Your team stores training datasets in Cloud Storage as individual CSV files (100K files, 5GB total). Training jobs on Vertex AI spend 45 minutes just reading data before training starts. What storage optimization would most improve training start time?
opts: Convert CSV files to TFRecord or Parquet format with optimal file sizes (100-200MB per file)
Enable Cloud Storage Turbo replication for faster reads
Store files in Cloud Storage buckets located in the same region as training
Use Cloud Storage FUSE to mount the bucket directly
explain: Converting to TFRecord or Parquet with larger file sizes dramatically reduces I/O overhead. Fewer, larger files mean fewer API calls, better sequential read performance, and efficient parallelization. This is the standard best practice for ML training data. Turbo replication (option B) helps with multi-region scenarios but doesn't address the file count issue. Same-region storage (option C) is good practice but doesn't solve the fundamental problem. FUSE (option D) adds overhead and isn't recommended for training.
concept: Store training data as TFRecord or Parquet with 100-200MB file sizes. Fewer, larger files reduce I/O overhead and improve training start time. Convert CSV to binary formats.

q: You've deployed a Vertex AI Pipeline that runs nightly to retrain a model. The pipeline succeeded for 30 days, then suddenly started failing at the training step with 'Out of Memory' errors. The training data volume increased from 10GB to 50GB. What is the most appropriate fix?
opts: Modify the training component to use a machine type with more memory, such as n1-highmem-16
Implement data sampling to reduce the dataset back to 10GB
Split the pipeline into multiple smaller pipelines
Increase the pipeline timeout limit to allow more processing time
explain: The root cause is insufficient memory for the larger dataset. The direct solution is to specify a larger machine type in the training component's configuration. Vertex AI Pipeline components accept machine_type parameter. Data sampling (option B) reduces model quality unnecessarily. Splitting pipelines (option C) doesn't address the memory issue. Increasing timeout (option D) is irrelevant for OOM errors.
concept: Vertex AI Pipeline component resource configuration: specify machine_type, accelerator_type, accelerator_count in component decorator. Scale resources as data grows.

q: Your deployed model on Vertex AI Endpoints is experiencing data drift. The model was trained on e-commerce transactions from 2023, and it's now mid-2024. You've set up Vertex AI Model Monitoring, which alerts you that input feature distributions have shifted significantly. What should be your immediate action?
opts: Retrain the model with recent data from 2024 and deploy the updated version
Increase the drift detection threshold to reduce false alerts
Switch to a different model architecture that's more robust to drift
Continue monitoring without changes since the model is still producing predictions
explain: Data drift indicates that the input distribution has changed, which typically degrades model performance. The correct response is to retrain with recent data that reflects the new distribution. Increasing the threshold (option B) ignores the real issue. Switching architectures (option C) doesn't address the stale training data. Continuing without action (option D) allows model performance to degrade further.
concept: Data drift detection workflow: Alert → Validate degradation → Retrain with recent data → Deploy. Monitor training-serving skew to ensure production distribution matches training.

q: You're setting up Vertex AI Model Monitoring for a fraud detection model. The model receives 10 million predictions per day, but you want to balance monitoring coverage with costs. How should you configure sampling?
opts: Configure a random sample rate (e.g., 10%) to monitor a representative subset while controlling costs
Monitor all predictions for the first hour of each day only
Monitor only predictions with confidence scores below 0.7
Disable sampling and monitor all predictions for accurate drift detection
explain: Random sampling provides statistically valid drift detection while reducing monitoring costs. A 10% sample from 10 million requests is 1 million predictions, which is more than sufficient for drift detection. Hourly monitoring (option B) may miss time-of-day patterns. Confidence-based sampling (option C) creates selection bias. Monitoring all predictions (option D) is unnecessarily expensive without improving detection quality at this scale.
concept: Vertex AI Model Monitoring sampling: Random sample rate balances cost and accuracy. 1-10% sampling is typically sufficient for drift detection on high-volume endpoints.

q: Your model monitoring alerts show concept drift: input distributions are stable, but model performance metrics (precision, recall) have degraded by 15% over two weeks. The model predicts customer churn for a subscription service. What is the most likely root cause?
opts: Customer behavior patterns have changed, making historical patterns less predictive of current churn
There's a bug in the model serving code that corrupts predictions
The input data pipeline is sending incorrectly formatted features
The model endpoint is experiencing latency issues
explain: Concept drift occurs when the relationship between features and target changes, even if feature distributions remain stable. In subscription services, customer behavior evolves (new competitors, economic changes, product updates) making historical patterns obsolete. Serving bugs (option B) would typically cause sudden failures, not gradual degradation. Incorrect features (option C) would cause data drift. Latency (option D) doesn't affect prediction accuracy.
concept: Concept drift = relationship between X and Y changes. Data drift = distribution of X changes. Concept drift requires retraining with recent labeled data to capture new patterns.

q: You need to configure Vertex AI Model Monitoring to detect training-serving skew for a tabular model with 45 features. The model was trained on data from January-March 2024. What baseline should you use for skew detection?
opts: Use the training dataset from January-March 2024 as the baseline to compare against production serving data
Use the first week of production serving data as the baseline
Use synthetic data generated to match expected production distribution
Use validation dataset predictions as the baseline
explain: Training-serving skew compares production serving data against the original training data distribution. The training dataset is the correct baseline because it represents what the model learned. Using serving data as baseline (option B) doesn't detect skew from training. Synthetic data (option C) doesn't reflect actual training distribution. Validation predictions (option D) are outputs, not input features.
concept: Training-serving skew: Compare production inputs against training data baseline. Detects if production data differs from what the model learned. Use training dataset as baseline.

q: Your Vertex AI Model Monitoring dashboard shows that for a numeric feature 'transaction_amount', the Jensen-Shannon divergence is 0.45 (threshold: 0.3). What does this indicate and how should you respond?
opts: The transaction_amount distribution in production differs significantly from training; investigate if this affects model performance and consider retraining
The feature is strongly correlated with the target; increase its weight in the model
The feature has missing values that need to be imputed
The monitoring service is misconfigured and reporting incorrect metrics
explain: Jensen-Shannon divergence measures distribution similarity (0=identical, 1=completely different). A value of 0.45 exceeding threshold 0.3 indicates significant distribution shift between training and production data. Investigate whether this affects model quality and retrain if performance has degraded. This metric doesn't indicate correlation (option B), missing values (option C), or misconfiguration (option D).
concept: JS divergence > threshold indicates distribution shift. Actions: 1) Check model performance metrics, 2) Investigate cause of shift, 3) Retrain if performance degraded. Typical threshold: 0.2-0.3.

q: You're deploying a model monitoring system that needs to detect drift within 1 hour of occurrence. The model serves 50,000 predictions per hour. What monitoring window configuration would best meet this requirement?
opts: Set monitoring window to 1 hour with evaluation frequency of 1 hour
Set monitoring window to 24 hours with evaluation frequency of 1 hour
Set monitoring window to 1 hour with evaluation frequency of 15 minutes
Set monitoring window to 15 minutes with evaluation frequency of 1 hour
explain: To detect drift within 1 hour, both the monitoring window and evaluation frequency should be 1 hour. This means: collect 1 hour of data, then evaluate. With 50K predictions/hour, this provides sufficient sample size. Option B's 24-hour window would delay detection. Option C would evaluate every 15 minutes but needs at least 1 hour of data for statistical validity. Option D's 15-minute window lacks sufficient samples (only ~12.5K).
concept: Model monitoring windows: Monitoring window = data collection period, Evaluation frequency = how often to check. Set both to detection latency requirement with sufficient sample size.

q: Your monitoring system detected prediction drift: the distribution of model outputs has changed significantly. Input feature distributions remain stable. What could cause this and what should you investigate?
opts: A new model version was deployed with different behavior, or a subtle bug in preprocessing; check deployment history and feature engineering logic
This indicates data drift and requires retraining with new data
The model endpoint is overloaded and serving cached predictions
This is normal model behavior and requires no action
explain: Prediction drift with stable inputs suggests changes in the model or preprocessing pipeline, not data drift. Common causes: new model deployment, changes in feature engineering, bugs in serving logic. Check deployment logs and compare preprocessing code. Data drift (option B) would show in input distributions. Caching (option C) wouldn't change distribution patterns. Prediction drift always warrants investigation (option D is incorrect).
concept: Prediction drift + stable inputs = model/pipeline change. Check: deployment history, feature engineering code, serving configuration. Different from data drift (input change) or concept drift (X-Y relationship change).

q: You need to set up alerting for model monitoring drift detection. Your fraud detection model has 20 features, and you want to avoid alert fatigue from noisy features. How should you configure alerts?
opts: Set alerts only for the top 5 most important features based on feature importance scores, and use a composite alert that requires multiple features to drift
Set individual alerts for all 20 features with the same threshold
Alert only when prediction drift is detected, ignoring feature-level drift
Set stricter thresholds (lower sensitivity) for all features to reduce alerts
explain: Focus monitoring on important features reduces noise while catching meaningful drift. Combine with composite alerts (e.g., alert when 2+ features drift or when weighted drift score exceeds threshold) to avoid false positives. Option B creates alert fatigue. Option C might miss important feature drift that doesn't immediately affect predictions. Option D reduces sensitivity uniformly, potentially missing real drift.
concept: Model monitoring alert strategy: Prioritize important features, use composite alerts (multiple features or weighted scores), set feature-specific thresholds based on importance.

q: Your team uses Vertex AI Model Monitoring and wants to integrate drift alerts with the incident response system. When drift is detected, you need to automatically trigger a pipeline to retrain the model. How should you implement this?
opts: Configure Vertex AI Model Monitoring to publish alerts to a Pub/Sub topic, then trigger a Cloud Function that starts the Vertex AI Pipeline
Use Cloud Monitoring to poll the Model Monitoring dashboard and trigger retraining when drift is detected
Configure Vertex AI Model Monitoring to directly trigger Vertex AI Pipelines
Set up a cron job to check monitoring metrics hourly and trigger retraining
explain: Vertex AI Model Monitoring can publish alerts to Pub/Sub topics. Create a Cloud Function subscriber that receives alerts and triggers the retraining pipeline via Vertex AI Pipelines API. This is event-driven and responsive. Polling (option B) adds delay. Direct pipeline triggering (option C) isn't supported. Cron jobs (option D) are not event-driven and may miss or delay alerts.
concept: Model monitoring alerting: Vertex AI Model Monitoring → Pub/Sub → Cloud Function → Action (retrain/notify). Event-driven response to drift detection.

q: You're monitoring a recommendation model that uses embedding-based features. The monitoring system should detect when user behavior embeddings drift from training distributions. What monitoring approach is most appropriate for high-dimensional embeddings?
opts: Use dimensionality reduction (PCA/UMAP) to project embeddings to lower dimensions, then monitor the reduced representation distributions
Monitor each embedding dimension independently using standard drift metrics
Calculate the mean embedding vector and monitor only the mean
Skip monitoring embeddings and only monitor the final recommendation outputs
explain: High-dimensional embeddings (e.g., 128-512 dimensions) are challenging to monitor directly. Reduce dimensions using PCA or UMAP to 2-3 dimensions while preserving distribution structure, then apply standard drift detection. Option B (independent dimensions) misses correlated changes and is computationally expensive. Option C (mean only) loses distribution information. Option D misses feature-level drift that may not yet affect outputs.
concept: Monitoring embeddings: Use dimensionality reduction (PCA/UMAP) → Monitor reduced space distribution. Captures embedding drift while avoiding high-dimensional monitoring challenges.

q: Your organization requires feature consistency between model training and serving. You have 50 features with complex transformations (rolling averages, time-based aggregations). How should you architecture your feature engineering to ensure training-serving consistency?
opts: Implement all feature transformations in Vertex AI Feature Store using Feature Views, then use the same Feature Store for both training and serving
Write transformation code twice: once in Python for training, once in SQL for serving
Use Dataflow to precompute all features and store them in BigQuery and Cloud Storage
Document the transformations and manually replicate them in training and serving code
explain: Vertex AI Feature Store with Feature Views allows you to define transformations once and apply them consistently for both training (batch) and serving (online). This eliminates training-serving skew from inconsistent implementations. Dual implementation (option B) is error-prone and hard to maintain. Precomputed features (option C) work for batch but don't support real-time serving. Documentation (option D) is prone to human error.
concept: Training-serving consistency: Use Vertex AI Feature Store Feature Views to define transformations once. Serves both batch (training) and online (serving) use cases with identical logic.

q: You need to serve features for online prediction with <10ms latency. Features are computed from real-time user events and include 30-day aggregations. The model receives 5,000 QPS. How should you architect feature serving?
opts: Use Vertex AI Feature Store online serving with materialized features, updated via streaming pipeline from Pub/Sub
Query BigQuery directly from the prediction service using cached queries
Store features in Cloud Storage and read them during each prediction
Compute features on-demand in the prediction service for each request
explain: Vertex AI Feature Store online serving is optimized for low-latency feature retrieval at high QPS. Maintain a streaming pipeline (Dataflow) that continuously updates feature store from events. Feature Store materializes aggregations, enabling <10ms lookups. BigQuery (option B) can't meet <10ms latency at 5K QPS. Cloud Storage reads (option C) are too slow. On-demand computation (option D) can't compute 30-day aggregations in real-time.
concept: Low-latency feature serving: Vertex AI Feature Store online serving + streaming pipeline for updates. Precompute and materialize complex aggregations. <10ms latency, high QPS support.

q: Your feature store has 200 features, but your model only uses 25 of them. The training pipeline takes 3 hours, with 45 minutes spent reading features from Vertex AI Feature Store. How can you optimize feature retrieval?
opts: Use Feature Store's entity type read API with feature_ids parameter to fetch only the 25 required features
Read all 200 features and filter them in the training code
Create a separate entity type containing only the 25 features used by the model
Export all features to BigQuery and query only needed columns
explain: Vertex AI Feature Store read APIs support selective feature retrieval via feature_ids parameter. Fetching only required features reduces data transfer and processing time. Reading all features (option B) wastes resources. Creating separate entity types (option C) duplicates data and complicates management. BigQuery export (option D) loses real-time updates and adds complexity.
concept: Feature Store optimization: Use selective feature retrieval via feature_ids parameter. Fetch only required features to reduce latency and costs. Applies to both online and batch serving.

q: You're designing a feature store strategy for multiple models (fraud detection, credit scoring, churn prediction) that share common customer features. Each model also has unique features. How should you organize features in Vertex AI Feature Store?
opts: Create one entity type for customers with feature groups for shared features and model-specific features, enabling feature reuse across models
Create separate entity types for each model with complete isolation
Store all features in a single flat feature list without groups
Use different Feature Store instances for each model
explain: Feature groups within a single entity type enable organization and reuse. Create groups like 'customer_demographics' (shared), 'fraud_signals' (fraud model), 'credit_signals' (credit model). Models fetch only needed groups. This promotes reuse while maintaining organization. Separate entity types (option B) duplicate shared features. Flat lists (option C) lack organization. Multiple instances (option D) prevent reuse and increase costs.
concept: Feature Store organization: One entity type per entity (customer, product) with feature groups for logical organization. Enables feature reuse across models while maintaining clear boundaries.

q: Your feature engineering pipeline computes point-in-time features for training. Features include transaction aggregations (30-day sum, count) that must reflect only data available at prediction time to avoid data leakage. How should you implement this?
opts: Use Vertex AI Feature Store's batch serving with timestamp parameter to retrieve point-in-time correct features for each training example
Compute features using the latest data regardless of timestamp
Manually filter out future data in post-processing
Use a fixed cutoff date for all training examples
