explain: Vertex AI Feature Store batch serving supports point-in-time correctness via timestamp parameters. For each training example, it retrieves features as they existed at that moment, preventing data leakage from future data. Using latest data (option B) causes leakage. Manual filtering (option C) is error-prone. Fixed cutoff (option D) limits training data to one point in time instead of enabling temporal range.
concept: Point-in-time correctness: Use Feature Store batch serving with timestamps to retrieve features as they existed at prediction time. Prevents data leakage during training.

q: Your online feature serving setup uses Vertex AI Feature Store, and you notice that feature values in production predictions differ from what's displayed in the Feature Store UI. What is the most likely cause and solution?
opts: There's a lag between feature updates and online serving propagation; verify feature sync lag metrics and adjust feature refresh frequency if needed
The model is using cached features from a previous deployment
The Feature Store is corrupted and needs to be recreated
The prediction service is reading from the wrong entity type
explain: Vertex AI Feature Store has eventual consistency between updates and online serving. Check sync lag metrics in monitoring. If features update every 5 minutes but sync takes 2 minutes, there's a 2-minute lag. Adjust update frequency or accept the lag. Model caching (option B) would show outdated but consistent values. Corruption (option C) would cause errors, not stale data. Wrong entity type (option D) would fail or return completely different values.
concept: Feature Store online serving consistency: Eventual consistency model with sync lag. Monitor sync lag metrics. Trade-off: higher update frequency = more cost, lower lag.

q: You need to version features in Vertex AI Feature Store as the feature engineering logic evolves. Models in production use different feature versions. How should you manage feature versioning?
opts: Use feature IDs with version suffixes (e.g., 'transaction_sum_v1', 'transaction_sum_v2') and maintain multiple versions simultaneously until all models migrate
Overwrite existing features when logic changes and retrain all models immediately
Create separate Feature Store instances for each version
Use Git to version feature code without versioning the features themselves
explain: Versioned feature IDs enable gradual migration. New models use v2, existing models continue using v1 until retrained. This prevents breaking production models. Overwriting (option B) breaks production models instantly. Separate instances (option C) are costly and complex. Git versioning (option D) doesn't help production serving access the right version.
concept: Feature versioning: Suffix feature IDs with versions (feature_v1, feature_v2). Maintain multiple versions simultaneously. Retire old versions after all models migrate. Gradual, safe migration.

q: Your feature store has 1 million customer entities, and each entity has 50 features. Training requires full historical feature snapshots for 2 years. Feature Store online serving costs are high. How should you optimize costs while maintaining serving performance?
opts: Use online serving only for active customers (recent activity), store historical features for inactive customers in BigQuery, accessed only for training
Reduce the number of features to lower storage costs
Disable online serving and read from BigQuery for all predictions
Decrease feature update frequency to reduce costs
explain: Most customers are inactive. Online serving costs scale with entity count. Store only active entities in online serving (fast, expensive), archive inactive entities to BigQuery (slow, cheap). Training can batch read from both. This optimizes cost/performance trade-off. Reducing features (option B) hurts model quality. BigQuery-only (option C) can't meet latency requirements. Lower update frequency (option D) doesn't reduce storage costs significantly.
concept: Feature Store cost optimization: Online serving for active entities only, offline storage (BigQuery) for inactive entities. Training reads from both. Reduces online serving costs dramatically.

q: You're implementing feature monitoring to detect when feature values in production serving deviate from expected ranges. You have 50 numerical features. What's the most effective monitoring strategy?
opts: Define expected ranges (min, max, mean, std) for each feature based on training data, then alert when serving features exceed 3 standard deviations
Monitor only the top 5 most important features
Log all feature values to BigQuery and manually review them weekly
Trust the model's prediction confidence scores to detect feature issues
explain: Statistical range monitoring is standard practice. Compute statistics from training data, then monitor serving features. Alert on outliers (e.g., >3σ). This detects data quality issues, pipeline bugs, and drift. Top-5 only (option B) misses issues in other features. Manual review (option C) is slow and doesn't scale. Confidence scores (option D) are model outputs, not feature quality metrics.
concept: Feature monitoring: Compute statistics (min, max, mean, std) from training data. Alert when serving features exceed thresholds (e.g., 3σ). Detects data quality issues and drift.

q: You need to implement a feature that computes 'average transaction amount in last 7 days' for online serving. The feature must be updated in real-time as transactions occur. Transactions arrive via Pub/Sub at 2,000 msgs/sec. How should you architect this?
opts: Use Dataflow to consume Pub/Sub messages, compute windowed aggregations, and update Vertex AI Feature Store online serving via streaming writes
Trigger a Cloud Function for each message to compute the aggregation and update Feature Store
Batch process transactions every hour and update Feature Store with BigQuery
Compute the aggregation on-demand during each prediction request
explain: Dataflow is designed for high-throughput streaming aggregations. Use windowed aggregations to compute rolling metrics and write to Feature Store. This handles 2K msgs/sec efficiently. Cloud Functions (option B) don't scale well for this throughput and have cold start latency. Hourly batch (option C) violates 'real-time' requirement. On-demand computation (option D) requires accessing raw transactions, which is slow and complex.
concept: Real-time feature updates: Pub/Sub → Dataflow (streaming aggregations) → Feature Store online serving. Handles high throughput, supports windowed/rolling aggregations. Use Dataflow, not Cloud Functions.

q: You're deploying a new model version to production using a blue-green deployment strategy. The blue environment serves the current model v1.0, and green will serve the new v2.0. How should you implement this on Vertex AI Endpoints?
opts: Create a new deployed model for v2.0 with 0% traffic, test it, then atomically shift 100% traffic from v1.0 to v2.0
Delete the v1.0 deployed model and deploy v2.0 in its place
Deploy both models to separate endpoints and use a load balancer to switch traffic
Use traffic splitting to gradually increase v2.0 traffic from 0% to 100% over several days
explain: Blue-green deployment on Vertex AI Endpoints: deploy new version with 0% traffic, validate with test requests, then atomically switch all traffic. This enables instant rollback if needed. Deleting v1.0 (option B) prevents rollback. Separate endpoints (option C) adds complexity and cost. Gradual splitting (option D) describes canary deployment, not blue-green.
concept: Blue-green deployment: Deploy new version (green) with 0% traffic alongside old (blue). Test green. Atomically switch 100% traffic. Instant rollback capability. Use Vertex AI Endpoints traffic splitting.

q: You're deploying a new model version using canary deployment. You've assigned 5% traffic to the canary (v2.0) and 95% to stable (v1.0). After 2 hours, the canary shows 0.5% higher error rate than stable. How should you proceed?
opts: Investigate the errors to determine if they're caused by model quality degradation or infrastructure issues; if model quality, rollback; if infrastructure, fix and retry
Immediately roll back to 100% stable version
Increase canary traffic to 10% to get more data
Continue monitoring for 24 hours before making a decision
explain: A 0.5% error rate increase needs investigation before action. Check error types: are they prediction quality issues (model problem) or serving errors (infrastructure problem)? If model quality degraded, rollback. If it's an infrastructure issue (e.g., resource limits), fix and retry. Immediate rollback (option B) is premature without understanding cause. Increasing traffic (option C) exposes more users to potential issues. Long monitoring (option D) delays necessary action.
concept: Canary deployment monitoring: Track error rates, latency, prediction quality. Investigate anomalies before action. Rollback if model quality degraded, fix infrastructure if serving issues. Don't wait passively.

q: Your production endpoint serves Model A (90% traffic) and Model B (10% traffic) in a canary configuration. Model B has 20% better accuracy but 50ms higher latency (150ms vs 100ms). Your SLA requires p99 latency <200ms. How should you proceed?
opts: Optimize Model B to reduce latency before increasing traffic, using techniques like model quantization, pruning, or better serving configuration
Increase Model B traffic to 50% since accuracy is more important than latency
Keep the current 10% traffic split permanently
Roll back Model B entirely and deploy only Model A
explain: Both accuracy and latency matter. Optimize Model B before scaling: try quantization (int8), pruning, or better serving configs (batch size, GPU utilization). If you can reduce Model B latency to <100ms, you can increase traffic while meeting SLAs. Ignoring latency (option B) violates SLAs. Permanent 10% (option C) wastes the accuracy improvement. Full rollback (option D) is premature before optimization attempts.
concept: Deployment trade-offs: Balance accuracy, latency, cost. When latency is high, optimize before scaling: quantization, pruning, serving config, hardware acceleration. Meet SLAs while improving quality.

q: You need to implement A/B testing for two model variants: a complex neural network (high accuracy, slow) and a gradient boosting model (lower accuracy, fast). You want to measure business impact (conversion rate) rather than model metrics. How should you structure this?
opts: Deploy both models on Vertex AI Endpoints with 50/50 traffic split, log user IDs with model version, track conversions in your application, then analyze statistical significance
Use Vertex AI Experiments to compare model accuracy metrics and deploy the better one
Deploy the neural network only and A/B test serving latency configurations
Let Vertex AI Endpoints automatically select the better model based on prediction confidence
explain: Business metric A/B testing requires: traffic splitting, user-model assignment tracking, business outcome measurement, and statistical analysis. Deploy both with traffic split, log which users saw which model, measure conversions, test for significance. Experiments (option B) only compare ML metrics, not business impact. Single model (option C) doesn't test the variants. Automatic selection (option D) isn't a feature and doesn't measure business metrics.
concept: A/B testing models: Deploy both on endpoint with traffic split → Log user-model assignments → Track business metrics (conversion, revenue) → Statistical significance test. Business impact, not just ML metrics.

q: Your organization requires gradual rollout of new models with automatic rollback if error rates exceed 2%. You're deploying a new model version. What deployment strategy should you implement on Vertex AI Endpoints?
opts: Canary deployment with Cloud Monitoring alerts on error rate; automate rollback using Cloud Functions triggered by alerts
Blue-green deployment with manual monitoring
Shadow deployment to compare models without affecting users
Direct deployment of the new model with rollback plan documentation
explain: Canary deployment meets the gradual rollout requirement. Implement Cloud Monitoring metrics on endpoint error rates, create alert policies for >2% error rate, trigger Cloud Functions that automatically adjust traffic back to stable version. Blue-green (option B) is all-or-nothing, not gradual. Shadow (option C) doesn't route real traffic. Direct deployment (option D) doesn't meet gradual requirement.
concept: Automated canary rollback: Cloud Monitoring metrics → Alert policy (error rate > threshold) → Cloud Function (adjust traffic via Vertex AI API) → Rollback. Automated safety for gradual rollouts.

q: You're implementing shadow deployment to validate a new model before production. The shadow model receives the same inputs as production but its predictions aren't returned to users. How should you implement this on GCP?
opts: Deploy both models on Vertex AI Endpoints, use prediction request logging, send requests to both models, compare predictions offline, return only production model results
Deploy shadow model on a separate endpoint and duplicate traffic using a load balancer
Use Vertex AI Endpoints traffic splitting with 99/1 split
Deploy shadow model in a different GCP project for isolation
explain: Implement shadow deployment by sending requests to both models on the same endpoint but only returning production predictions. Enable prediction logging to capture both results for offline comparison. This tests serving performance and generates comparison data. Load balancer duplication (option B) is complex and doesn't guarantee identical inputs. Traffic splitting (option C) exposes users to shadow model. Separate project (option D) adds complexity without benefits.
concept: Shadow deployment: Both models receive requests, only production model returns results. Log both predictions for offline comparison. Tests serving performance without user impact.

q: Your model endpoint traffic follows a diurnal pattern: 500 QPS during business hours, 50 QPS at night. Autoscaling is enabled, but you experience cold start latency spikes when traffic increases in the morning. How should you optimize this?
opts: Configure minimum replica count to handle typical morning traffic volume, accepting slightly higher off-peak costs for better user experience
Disable autoscaling and use a fixed large instance count
Reduce maximum replica count to limit costs
Use preemptible VMs for cost savings
explain: Set min replicas to handle the morning ramp-up without cold starts. For 500 QPS, if each replica handles 50 QPS, set min replicas to 10. This balances cost (paying for idle capacity at night) against user experience (no morning latency spikes). Fixed large count (option B) wastes money 20+ hours/day. Reducing max replicas (option C) causes capacity issues at peak. Preemptible VMs (option D) aren't supported for Vertex AI Endpoints.
concept: Endpoint autoscaling: Set min replicas to handle predictable ramp-ups without cold starts. Trade off idle capacity cost for better UX. Monitor replica utilization and adjust.

q: You need to deploy three model versions (v1, v2, v3) to the same endpoint with traffic split: v1=70%, v2=20%, v3=10%. Each version requires different resource configurations (v3 needs GPUs). How should you configure this on Vertex AI Endpoints?
opts: Deploy three separate deployed models to the same endpoint, each with its own machine type and accelerator configuration, then set traffic percentages
Deploy all versions using the same machine type (the highest required)
Create three separate endpoints and use Cloud Load Balancer for traffic splitting
Deploy only the version with highest traffic and gradually replace others
explain: Vertex AI Endpoints support multiple deployed models with independent resource configurations. Deploy each version separately specifying machine_type and accelerator, then set traffic percentages. This optimizes cost (only v3 uses GPUs) while enabling multi-variant serving. Uniform machine type (option B) wastes money on unnecessary GPUs. Separate endpoints (option C) adds complexity. Single version (option D) defeats the purpose of multi-variant testing.
concept: Multi-variant deployment: Deploy multiple models to one endpoint with independent resource configs. Each model specifies machine_type, accelerators. Set traffic split across models. Optimize per-variant resources.

q: Your model endpoint serves predictions at 1,000 QPS with p50 latency of 50ms and p99 latency of 500ms. Investigation shows that p99 requests wait in queue for 400ms. How should you optimize serving configuration?
opts: Increase the number of replicas to reduce queueing time for high-percentile requests
Decrease batch size to reduce per-request processing time
Add GPU accelerators to speed up inference
Implement request timeout to drop slow requests
explain: High p99 latency with significant queue time indicates insufficient serving capacity during peaks. Add replicas to increase throughput and reduce queueing. The p50 of 50ms shows individual predictions are fast; the issue is capacity. Decreasing batch size (option B) may increase latency overall. GPUs (option C) help compute-bound models, but this is queue-bound. Timeouts (option D) drop requests instead of serving them.
concept: Serving latency optimization: p50 vs p99 gap indicates capacity issues. High queue time → Add replicas. High inference time → Optimize model (quantization, batch, GPU). Monitor queue depth.

q: You're conducting hyperparameter tuning for a deep learning model using Vertex AI Vizier. The search space has 5 hyperparameters, and you have budget for 200 trials. What search algorithm should you use?
opts: Bayesian optimization, which efficiently explores the search space using prior trial results to guide subsequent trials
Grid search to exhaustively cover all hyperparameter combinations
Random search to uniformly sample the space
Manual tuning based on domain expertise
explain: Bayesian optimization (default in Vizier) is most efficient for continuous search spaces with limited budget. It builds a probabilistic model of the objective function and uses it to select promising trials, typically finding optimal regions in 50-200 trials. Grid search (option B) is infeasible (5 params with even 4 values each = 1,024 combinations). Random search (option C) is less efficient than Bayesian. Manual tuning (option D) doesn't scale.
concept: Vertex AI Vizier algorithm choice: Bayesian optimization for continuous spaces (default, efficient). Grid search only for small discrete spaces. Bayesian finds optima in 50-200 trials.

q: Your hyperparameter tuning job on Vertex AI shows that 60% of trials fail early due to NaN losses. The remaining 40% complete successfully. How should you adjust the tuning configuration to improve efficiency?
opts: Enable early stopping with median stopping rule to terminate unpromising trials faster, and adjust hyperparameter ranges to avoid unstable regions (e.g., learning rate)
Increase the number of trials to get more successful completions
Disable parallel trial execution to run one trial at a time
Reduce the search space to a single hyperparameter
explain: NaN losses indicate unstable training, often from extreme hyperparameters (high learning rate, wrong initialization). Adjust search ranges to exclude unstable regions, and enable early stopping to kill doomed trials quickly, freeing resources for better trials. Increasing trials (option B) wastes budget on failures. Sequential trials (option C) slows tuning without fixing failures. Single parameter (option D) misses important interactions.
concept: Hyperparameter tuning troubleshooting: High failure rate → Adjust search ranges to stable regions, enable early stopping. Check learning rate, initialization, batch size. Tune ranges iteratively.

q: You're tuning a gradient boosting model with hyperparameters: learning_rate [0.01-0.3], num_trees [50-500], max_depth [3-10]. You have budget for 100 trials. What search strategy is most appropriate?
opts: Grid search with carefully selected values: learning_rate=[0.01, 0.03, 0.1, 0.3], num_trees=[50, 100, 200, 500], max_depth=[3, 5, 7, 10], totaling 64 combinations
Bayesian optimization for continuous exploration
Random search across the full continuous ranges
Manual tuning starting from default values
explain: For gradient boosting, the search space is relatively small and discrete values are meaningful. Grid search with 64 well-chosen combinations is feasible within 100-trial budget and ensures systematic coverage. Bayesian optimization (option B) is overkill for this small space. Random search (option C) may miss important combinations. Manual tuning (option D) is less systematic.
concept: Hyperparameter search strategy: Grid search for small discrete spaces (<100 combos). Bayesian for large/continuous spaces. Tree models often work well with grid search.

q: Your Vizier hyperparameter tuning study shows that the best trial has learning_rate=0.001 and all trials with learning_rate>0.01 performed poorly. This suggests the optimal value might be even lower. How should you refine the search?
opts: Create a new study with narrower search range around the promising region: learning_rate [0.0001-0.005], keeping other parameters the same
Continue the existing study with more trials
Restart the study with a wider range [0.00001-1.0]
Accept learning_rate=0.001 as optimal without further search
explain: When search results concentrate in one region of the space, refine that region with a new focused study. Narrow the learning_rate range to [0.0001-0.005] to explore lower values. This is iterative refinement. Continuing the existing study (option B) keeps searching the already-explored large range. Widening (option C) adds more low-value regions. Accepting 0.001 (option D) may miss the true optimum at 0.0005 or 0.0001.
concept: Iterative hyperparameter tuning: Initial broad search → Identify promising region → Refined focused search. Narrow ranges around best results. 2-3 iterations typical.

q: You're using Vertex AI Vizier with 10 parallel trials. After 50 trials, the study hasn't converged and improvement has plateaued. What should you check and adjust?
opts: Review trial completion status to ensure trials are finishing successfully, check if early stopping is too aggressive, and verify the objective metric is being reported correctly
Increase parallel trials to 20 for faster convergence
Extend the maximum number of trials to 500
Switch from Bayesian to random search
explain: Plateauing suggests issues with the tuning setup. Check: Are trials completing? Is early stopping killing promising trials too soon? Is the objective metric correct (lower is better vs higher is better)? These issues are common. More parallel trials (option B) doesn't help if trials aren't informing the search properly. More trials (option C) wastes budget if the setup is broken. Switching algorithms (option D) doesn't address root cause.
concept: Vizier troubleshooting: Plateau → Check trial completion, early stopping config, objective metric direction, search ranges. Fix setup before adding more trials.

q: Your team wants to tune hyperparameters for multiple models (random forest, XGBoost, neural network) to select the best model type and configuration. How should you structure the hyperparameter tuning in Vertex AI?
opts: Create separate Vizier studies for each model type with appropriate hyperparameters, compare best results across studies, then select the best overall
Create a single study with a categorical 'model_type' parameter
Tune each model manually and compare results
Always use neural networks since they're most flexible
explain: Different model types have completely different hyperparameter spaces, so separate studies are clearest. Tune each model type independently to find its best configuration, then compare the best configurations. Single study with model_type (option B) forces shared hyperparameters or requires conditional logic that most tools don't support well. Manual tuning (option C) is inefficient. Assuming neural networks (option D) ignores potential advantages of tree models.
concept: Multi-model hyperparameter tuning: Separate Vizier studies per model type. Find best config for each type, then compare across types. Different models have different hyperparameter spaces.

q: You're tuning a neural network with hyperparameters: learning_rate, batch_size, num_layers, hidden_units. The search space is large. How can you reduce tuning cost while maintaining search quality?
opts: Use early stopping to terminate unpromising trials quickly, train on a subset of data for initial search, then fine-tune best candidates on full data
Reduce the search space to a single hyperparameter
Use grid search with very coarse granularity
Run all trials on preemptible VMs only
explain: Multi-stage tuning reduces cost: (1) Coarse search on subset data with early stopping, (2) Fine-tune top-k candidates on full data. This finds promising regions quickly, then invests in detailed evaluation. Single parameter (option B) misses important interactions. Coarse grid (option C) may miss optimal regions. Preemptible VMs (option D) cause trial interruptions.
concept: Cost-effective hyperparameter tuning: Early stopping + train on data subset for initial search → Fine-tune top candidates on full data. Two-stage approach balances cost and quality.

q: Your Vizier study uses a metric 'validation_accuracy' as the objective. After 100 trials, you discover the metric was incorrectly computed (validation set had data leakage). What should you do?
opts: Abandon the study, fix the metric calculation, and start a new study from scratch
Continue the study and manually adjust the results
Keep the top trials and create a new study for fine-tuning
Stop the study and use the best trial despite the error
explain: When the objective metric is wrong, all trial evaluations are invalid and comparisons are meaningless. Fix the metric calculation and restart. Continuing (option B) compounds the error. Using flawed results (options C, D) risks deploying a poorly tuned model. The time spent is a sunk cost; starting fresh with correct metrics is the only valid path.
concept: Hyperparameter tuning validation: Verify objective metric correctness early. If metric is wrong, restart study. Invalid evaluations make all results meaningless. Sunk cost fallacy.

q: You want to tune hyperparameters for a model that takes 8 hours to train. Running 100 trials would take 800 hours sequentially. How should you accelerate hyperparameter tuning?
opts: Use parallel trials in Vertex AI Vizier (e.g., 20 parallel workers) to reduce wall-clock time to ~40 hours, and enable early stopping to terminate unpromising trials
Reduce the number of trials to 10
Train on a smaller dataset to reduce training time
Use a simpler model that trains faster
explain: Parallelization directly reduces wall-clock time. With 20 parallel workers, 100 trials complete in ~5 iterations (100/20), taking ~40 hours plus overhead. Add early stopping to further reduce time. Reducing trials (option B) reduces search quality. Smaller dataset (option C) may not reflect full-data performance. Simpler model (option D) changes the problem.
concept: Accelerate hyperparameter tuning: Parallel trials (10-20 workers), early stopping, multi-fidelity (train on subset first). Balance wall-clock time, cost, and search quality.

q: You're designing a custom training job on Vertex AI that uses TensorFlow with a custom data loader. The training script needs access to credentials to read from a private Cloud Storage bucket. How should you provide credentials securely?
opts: Use the default Compute Engine service account associated with the training job, grant it Storage Object Viewer role, and access GCS using Google Cloud libraries without explicit credentials
Generate a service account key, download it as JSON, and include it in the training container
Hard-code credentials in the training script
Pass credentials as environment variables in the job configuration
explain: Vertex AI training jobs run with a service account identity (default or custom). Grant the service account appropriate IAM roles, and Google Cloud client libraries automatically use that identity. No explicit credentials needed. Service account keys (option B) are security risks and should be avoided. Hard-coding (option C) is terrible security practice. Environment variables (option D) expose credentials in logs and configs.
concept: Vertex AI training authentication: Use service account identity (default or custom), grant IAM roles. No explicit credentials needed. Google Cloud SDKs use automatic authentication.

q: Your custom training job uses 4 GPUs for distributed training. The job fails with 'CUDA out of memory' errors. The model size is 2GB and each GPU has 16GB memory. What is the most likely cause?
opts: Batch size is too large, causing activation memory to exceed GPU capacity; reduce global batch size or implement gradient accumulation
The model is too large for the GPUs
GPUs are not configured correctly in the job specification
There's a memory leak in TensorFlow
explain: With a 2GB model on 16GB GPUs, model size isn't the issue. During training, activations, gradients, and optimizer states consume memory. Large batches increase activation memory. Reduce per-GPU batch size or use gradient accumulation to maintain effective batch size. Model size (option B) is clearly not the issue (2GB << 16GB). GPU configuration (option C) would cause different errors. Memory leaks (option D) cause gradual memory growth, not immediate OOM.
concept: GPU OOM troubleshooting: Model fits but OOM during training → Reduce batch size or use gradient accumulation. Activation memory = f(batch size, sequence length, model depth).

q: You're training a model using distributed training with parameter server strategy on 4 machines. Training is slower than expected, and monitoring shows that parameter server CPU utilization is at 95% while worker GPUs are at 40%. What optimization should you apply?
opts: Increase the number of parameter servers or use a machine type with more CPU cores for parameter servers
Reduce the number of workers to decrease load on parameter servers
Switch to a different distributed training strategy like MirroredStrategy
Increase GPU count per worker
explain: Parameter server bottleneck (high CPU) causes workers to idle waiting for gradients/parameters. Add more parameter servers or use larger machines for existing servers to distribute the load. Reducing workers (option B) reduces parallelism unnecessarily. MirroredStrategy (option C) is for single-machine multi-GPU, not multi-machine. More GPUs per worker (option D) increases load on already-saturated parameter servers.
concept: Distributed training bottlenecks: Parameter server CPU at 100%, workers idle → Add parameter servers or increase their CPU. Worker GPU idle → Optimize data pipeline or reduce batch size.

q: Your custom training container needs several large dependencies (5GB total). Building the container takes 20 minutes, and you iterate frequently during development. How should you optimize the container build process?
opts: Structure Dockerfile with stable dependencies first, code last; use Docker layer caching to avoid rebuilding dependencies; consider using a base image with pre-installed dependencies
Build the container from scratch every time to ensure consistency
Install dependencies at runtime using pip install in the training script
Reduce dependencies to speed up builds
explain: Docker layer caching reuses unchanged layers. Structure Dockerfile: base image → system packages → Python dependencies → application code. When code changes, only the last layer rebuilds. Also consider a custom base image with pre-installed dependencies. Building from scratch (option B) is slow. Runtime installation (option C) makes every training job slow. Reducing dependencies (option D) may not be feasible.
concept: Docker build optimization: Stable layers first (dependencies), volatile layers last (code). Enable layer caching. Use pre-built base images for common dependencies. Reduces iteration time.

q: You're running distributed training for a computer vision model with data parallelism across 8 GPUs. The training step time is 100ms, but you observe significant variation (80ms-150ms) between steps. What is the most likely cause?
o pts: Straggler effects where one GPU finishes slower than others, causing synchronization delays; investigate load imbalance in data distribution or check for hardware issues
explain: In synchronous data parallel training, all workers must finish before proceeding. High variance indicates stragglers: one GPU takes longer due to harder examples, hardware issues, or load imbalance. Check data distribution uniformity and hardware health. Network bandwidth (option B) affects all steps similarly, not causing high variance. Architecture (option C) doesn't explain variance. Batch size (option D) affects mean time, not variance.
concept: Distributed training variance: Synchronous training waits for slowest worker (straggler). High variance → Check data distribution balance, hardware health, system load. Use async training or gradient accumulation.

q: Your training job uses mixed precision training (fp16) to speed up computation. You notice that the loss becomes NaN after a few hundred steps. What is the most likely cause and solution?
opts: Gradient underflow or overflow in fp16; enable loss scaling to adjust gradients into representable fp16 range, or use automatic mixed precision (AMP) which handles this automatically
The learning rate is too high
Mixed precision is incompatible with your model architecture
GPU doesn't support fp16 properly
explain: fp16 has limited range (~6e-8 to 65,504). Gradients can underflow (become 0) or overflow (become inf), causing NaN. Loss scaling multiplies gradients before backprop to keep them in fp16 range. TensorFlow/PyTorch AMP handles this automatically. High learning rate (option B) causes instability but not necessarily NaN. Architecture incompatibility (option C) is rare. GPU support (option D) isn't the issue on Vertex AI GPUs.
concept: Mixed precision training: fp16 has limited range. Use loss scaling or automatic mixed precision (AMP) to prevent gradient underflow/overflow. AMP automatically manages scaling.

q: You need to train a model that requires 100GB of GPU memory, but the largest available GPU (A100) has only 80GB. How should you proceed?
opts: Use model parallelism to split the model across multiple GPUs, or use gradient checkpointing to reduce memory usage by recomputing activations
Reduce the model size until it fits on one GPU
Use TPUs instead of GPUs
Increase batch size to improve memory efficiency
explain: For models larger than single GPU memory: (1) Model parallelism splits layers across GPUs, or (2) Gradient checkpointing trades computation for memory by recomputing activations during backprop instead of storing them. Reducing model size (option B) may hurt performance. TPUs (option C) have similar memory limits. Increasing batch size (option D) increases memory usage, not reduces it.
concept: Large model training: Model > GPU memory → Model parallelism (split layers across GPUs) or gradient checkpointing (trade compute for memory). Also: pipeline parallelism, offloading.

q: Your training job writes checkpoints to Cloud Storage every 1,000 steps. Checkpoint writes take 30 seconds and block training. How should you optimize this?
opts: Use asynchronous checkpoint saving so training continues while checkpoints are being written to Cloud Storage
Reduce checkpoint frequency to every 5,000 steps
Write checkpoints to local disk and copy to Cloud Storage after training
Use smaller checkpoint sizes by saving only model weights without optimizer state
explain: TensorFlow and PyTorch support async checkpoint saving: training continues on the next step while the previous checkpoint writes in the background. This eliminates the 30s blocking time. Reducing frequency (option B) helps but sacrifices recovery points. Local disk (option C) risks data loss if training fails. Saving only weights (option D) prevents resuming training with optimizer state.
concept: Checkpoint optimization: Use asynchronous checkpoint saving to avoid blocking training. Training continues while checkpoint writes to storage. TensorFlow/PyTorch support async saves.

q: You're implementing custom training with distributed training strategy. The training data is 1TB stored in Cloud Storage as 10,000 TFRecord files. How should you distribute data loading across workers?
opts: Use tf.data with automatic sharding (AutoShardPolicy) so each worker reads a disjoint subset of files, or manually shard files and assign to workers
Have all workers read all files
Assign files to workers randomly on each epoch
Pre-split data into worker-specific directories
explain: Distributed training requires each worker to process different data. Use tf.data.experimental.AutoShardPolicy.DATA or FILE to automatically assign file shards to workers, or manually shard using worker_id and num_workers. All workers reading all data (option B) causes duplicate training. Random assignment (option C) can cause overlap and missing data. Pre-splitting (option D) is manual and inflexible.
concept: Distributed data loading: Use tf.data AutoShardPolicy or manual sharding. Each worker reads disjoint data subset. FILE sharding for TFRecords. Prevents duplicate training on same examples.

q: You need to evaluate whether a new model variant (B) is significantly better than the current model (A). You've collected 10,000 prediction pairs. Variant B has 72% accuracy and variant A has 70% accuracy. How should you determine if this 2% difference is statistically significant?
opts: Use McNemar's test for paired binary classifications to test if the difference is statistically significant, or use bootstrap confidence intervals
Compare accuracy directly; 2% is significant enough
A/B test on 10% of users first
Use a t-test to compare the means
explain: For paired predictions (same examples), use McNemar's test which accounts for correlation. It tests if the disagreement patterns (A right B wrong vs A wrong B right) are significantly different. Alternatively, bootstrap confidence intervals. Direct comparison (option B) ignores statistical significance. A/B test (option C) is deployment, not analysis. T-test (option D) assumes independence, which paired predictions violate.
concept: Model comparison statistical test: Paired predictions → McNemar's test or bootstrap confidence intervals. Tests if disagreement pattern is significant. Accounts for correlation between predictions.

q: You're running an A/B test comparing two models. Model A serves 50% of traffic (5,000 users) with 10% conversion rate. Model B serves 50% of traffic (5,000 users) with 11% conversion rate. How should you evaluate if Model B is significantly better?
opts: Use a two-proportion z-test to calculate p-value and confidence interval for the conversion rate difference between Model A and B
Deploy Model B to 100% since it has higher conversion rate
Continue collecting data for one more week before analysis
Compare daily conversion rates and deploy if B wins more days
explain: Two-proportion z-test determines if the difference in conversion rates (11% vs 10%) is statistically significant given sample sizes. Calculate p-value: if p<0.05, the difference is significant. With 5,000 users each, 1% absolute difference is likely significant but requires testing. Immediate deployment (option B) ignores significance. More data (option C) may be unnecessary. Daily comparison (option D) isn't a valid statistical test.
concept: A/B test significance: Two-proportion z-test for conversion rates. Calculate p-value and confidence interval. Sufficient sample size: need ~1,000-10,000 users per variant depending on effect size.

q: Your A/B test shows Model B has 0.5% higher conversion rate than Model A with p-value of 0.08 (not statistically significant at 0.05 level). The business team wants to deploy Model B anyway because any improvement is valuable. How should you advise?
opts: Recommend continuing the test to collect more data for sufficient statistical power, or accept higher significance threshold (e.g., 0.10) if business context justifies it
Deploy Model B immediately since it shows improvement
Reject Model B entirely and keep Model A
Run a new test with different traffic allocation
explain: p=0.08 is marginally non-significant. Options: (1) Collect more data to increase power and get p<0.05, or (2) Use 0.10 significance threshold if business context (low risk, high value) justifies accepting more false positives. Immediate deployment (option B) risks deploying a model that isn't actually better. Outright rejection (option C) ignores the marginal result. New test (option D) doesn't address the power issue.
concept: A/B test interpretation: p-value near significance threshold → Collect more data or adjust α based on business context (risk tolerance, value of improvement). Balance statistical rigor and business needs.

q: You're implementing multi-armed bandit testing for three model variants. After 1,000 users, the algorithm has assigned 10% to Model A, 15% to Model B, and 75% to Model C. What does this indicate?
opts: Model C is performing best so far, and the algorithm is exploiting it while still exploring A and B at reduced rates
The algorithm is broken and should assign traffic equally
Model C should be deployed to 100% immediately
Models A and B should be removed from the test
explain: Multi-armed bandit algorithms balance exploration (trying all arms) with exploitation (favoring better-performing arms). 75% to Model C indicates it's the current best, but 10-15% still goes to A and B to monitor performance and detect changes. This is correct behavior. Equal traffic (option B) is A/B testing, not bandit. Immediate 100% deployment (option C) stops learning. Removing variants (option D) eliminates adaptation ability.
concept: Multi-armed bandit: Dynamically allocates traffic to better-performing variants while maintaining exploration. Traffic allocation shifts toward winners but doesn't eliminate losers entirely. Adapts faster than fixed A/B tests.

q: Your bandit algorithm has been running for 2 weeks. Initially Model A had 60% traffic and Model B had 40%. Now both have 50% traffic and performance metrics show Model A is better. What could explain this?
opts: The bandit algorithm is still exploring; increase exploitation parameter to favor Model A more strongly, or check if external factors (seasonality, user segments) affect relative performance
The algorithm is broken and should be restarted
Model B has actually improved and caught up to Model A
This is expected convergence behavior
explain: If Model A is objectively better but traffic is equal, the algorithm is either still exploring or doesn't have sufficient signal to differentiate. Increase exploitation (reduce epsilon/temperature) to favor winners more. Also check if performance differs by segment or time. Algorithm failure (option B) is unlikely. Real improvement (option C) should show in metrics. Equal traffic (option D) isn't correct convergence when performance differs.
concept: Bandit tuning: Adjust exploration-exploitation balance (epsilon, temperature). More exploration = slower convergence, less risk of local optima. More exploitation = faster convergence, more risk.

q: You need to compare three model variants on both conversion rate and latency (multi-objective optimization). Variant A has highest conversion but highest latency. How should you approach this evaluation?
opts: Define a combined objective function that weights conversion and latency based on business priorities (e.g., score = conversion - 0.1*latency_ms), or use Pareto frontier analysis
Choose the variant with highest conversion regardless of latency
Choose the variant with lowest latency regardless of conversion
Run separate A/B tests for each metric
explain: Multi-objective optimization requires trading off metrics. Define a combined score reflecting business priorities, or use Pareto frontier to identify non-dominated solutions. For example, if 100ms latency costs 0.1% conversion, weight accordingly. Prioritizing one metric (options B, C) ignores the other. Separate tests (option D) don't address the trade-off.
concept: Multi-objective A/B testing: Define combined metric weighted by business value, or use Pareto frontier (non-dominated solutions). Example: score = conversion - λ*latency. Choose based on business priorities.

q: You're using Vertex AI Explainable AI to understand feature attributions for a tabular model. The explanation shows that a feature 'customer_id' has high attribution scores. What does this likely indicate?
opts: The model is likely overfitting to customer_id, which is a high-cardinality identifier that shouldn't be predictive; investigate and likely remove this feature
customer_id is an important feature and should be retained
The explanation service is misconfigured
This indicates the model is working correctly
explain: High attribution for identifiers like customer_id indicates memorization/overfitting, not learning. The model is using individual IDs rather than generalizable patterns. This fails on new customers. Remove identifier features or aggregate them into meaningful features. It's not a useful feature (option B). Misconfiguration (option C) would cause errors, not bad attributions. This is a red flag, not correct behavior (option D).
concept: Feature attribution interpretation: High attribution to identifiers (ID, timestamp) = overfitting/memorization. Remove or aggregate such features. Model should learn from generalizable patterns, not memorize.

q: Your Vertex AI explanation request for a prediction takes 5 seconds, while the prediction itself takes 50ms. You need to serve explanations in production at high QPS. How should you optimize this?
opts: Generate explanations asynchronously for sampled requests rather than all requests, or use explanation metadata API for batch explanations
Disable explanations in production and only use them for debugging
Increase the model serving resources
Use a simpler explanation method like feature importance instead of per-prediction attributions
explain: Explanation computation is expensive (100x slower than prediction). For high QPS, compute explanations for a sample (e.g., 1%) asynchronously and store them, or batch explanation requests. Disabling entirely (option B) loses production explainability. More serving resources (option C) helps but doesn't address the fundamental cost. Global feature importance (option D) doesn't explain individual predictions.
concept: Production explainability: Generate explanations for sampled requests only (1-10%). Async/batch explanation computation. Trade-off: coverage vs latency. Full per-prediction explanations too expensive for high QPS.

q: You're using SHAP values to explain a model that predicts house prices. For one prediction, 'num_bedrooms' has SHAP value of +$50K and 'crime_rate' has SHAP value of -$30K. How should you interpret this?
opts: This prediction is $50K higher due to num_bedrooms and $30K lower due to crime_rate compared to the base value (average prediction)
The model thinks num_bedrooms is more important than crime_rate in general
The prediction increased by $20K total due to these two features
These are the actual feature values in scaled units
explain: SHAP values are additive attribution: base_value + sum(SHAP values) = prediction. Each SHAP value shows that feature's contribution to moving the prediction from average. +$50K means num_bedrooms pushes prediction $50K above base, -$30K means crime_rate pushes $30K below. This is per-prediction, not general importance (option B). They sum to contribute $20K (option C), but each effect is interpreted separately. They're attribution scores, not feature values (option D).
concept: SHAP value interpretation: Individual feature contribution to prediction. Base value (average) + SHAP values = prediction. Positive = increases prediction, negative = decreases. Additive and instance-specific.

q: Your team wants to debug why a fraud detection model classified a transaction as fraudulent. The model has 50 features. What explainability approach should you use?
opts: Use Vertex AI Explainable AI with integrated gradients or SHAP to get feature attributions for this specific prediction, showing which features most influenced the fraud classification
Print global feature importance scores
Review all 50 feature values manually
Retrain the model and see if the prediction changes
explain: For instance-level explanation ('why this prediction'), use per-prediction attribution methods like Integrated Gradients (neural networks) or SHAP (any model). These show which features most influenced this specific decision. Global importance (option B) doesn't explain individual predictions. Manual review (option C) doesn't show impact. Retraining (option D) doesn't explain the current model.
concept: Instance-level explainability: Use per-prediction attributions (Integrated Gradients, SHAP) to explain individual predictions. Shows which features influenced this specific decision. Different from global feature importance.

q: You need to implement MLOps CI/CD for model training and deployment. Code changes should trigger automated training, evaluation, and deployment if quality thresholds are met. How should you architect this on GCP?
opts: Use Cloud Build triggered by Git commits to run Vertex AI Pipeline, evaluate model metrics, and conditionally deploy to Vertex AI Endpoints using pipeline gates
Manually trigger training and deployment for each code change
Use Cloud Functions to trigger training on every commit
Set up a cron job to retrain models daily regardless of code changes
explain: CI/CD for ML: Git commit → Cloud Build → Vertex AI Pipeline (train, evaluate) → Conditional deployment (if metrics > threshold). This automates the full workflow with quality gates. Manual triggering (option B) isn't CI/CD. Cloud Functions (option C) can trigger training but lack orchestration for evaluation and deployment. Cron jobs (option D) don't respond to code changes and waste resources.
concept: ML CI/CD: Git commit → Cloud Build → Vertex AI Pipeline (train+evaluate+deploy) → Conditional deployment. Automated testing, quality gates, reproducible deployments. Infrastructure as code.

q: Your team has 10 data scientists training models independently. Training configs (hyperparameters, data paths) are scattered across notebooks and scripts, making experiments hard to reproduce. How should you standardize experiment tracking?
opts: Mandate use of Vertex AI Experiments for all training runs, with required logging of hyperparameters, metrics, and artifacts; provide template scripts
Ask data scientists to document experiments in a shared spreadsheet
Store configs in a Git repository
Use individual notebooks without standardization to maintain flexibility
explain: Vertex AI Experiments provides centralized tracking with SDK integration. Standardize by requiring experiment initialization, parameter logging, and metric logging in all training code. Provide templates to reduce friction. Spreadsheets (option B) are manual and error-prone. Git (option C) tracks code but not runtime metrics and artifacts. No standardization (option D) maintains the problem.
concept: Experiment tracking standardization: Mandate centralized tracking system (Vertex AI Experiments). Provide templates. Log hyperparameters, metrics, artifacts. Enable comparison and reproducibility across team.

q: Your organization requires that all deployed models be traceable to source code, data version, and hyperparameters. How should you implement this traceability in your MLOps pipeline?
opts: Tag model artifacts with metadata including Git commit SHA, data version/path, hyperparameters, and training job ID; store in Model Registry
Store information in a separate documentation system
Include model lineage in the model file name
Trust data scientists to document this information manually
explain: Use Vertex AI Model Registry with custom metadata labels to tag models with lineage: git_sha, data_version, hyperparameters, training_job_id. Query-able and immutable. Separate docs (option B) become out-of-sync. Filename (option C) is limited and not query-able. Manual documentation (option D) is unreliable.
concept: Model lineage tracking: Tag model artifacts with metadata (git SHA, data version, hyperparameters, training job ID). Store in Model Registry. Enables reproducibility and auditing.

q: Your model training pipeline uses a dataset that is updated weekly. You need to ensure reproducibility: retraining with the same code should produce the same model even months later. How should you implement this?
opts: Version datasets with timestamps or content hashes, store in immutable storage, and reference specific dataset versions in training configs
Always use the latest dataset version
Trust that the dataset won't change significantly
Store datasets locally on training machines
explain: Version datasets explicitly: store with timestamp or content hash (e.g., gs://bucket/data/v20240101/), never overwrite. Reference specific versions in training code. Enables exact reproduction. Using latest data (option B) breaks reproducibility. Assuming stability (option C) is risky. Local storage (option D) doesn't provide shared access or durability.
concept: Data versioning for reproducibility: Immutable dataset versions with timestamps/hashes. Store in Cloud Storage. Reference specific versions in training code. Never overwrite. Enables exact reproduction.

q: Your ML pipeline includes data preprocessing, feature engineering, model training, and evaluation. You want to ensure reproducibility. What should you version control beyond the model code?
opts: Version control training code, preprocessing code, feature engineering code, dependency specifications (requirements.txt), Docker images (via tags), and pipeline definitions
Version control only the model training script
Version control only the final model artifacts
Reproducibility doesn't require version control if you use the same environment
explain: Reproducibility requires versioning all code that affects model outcomes: preprocessing, feature engineering, training, evaluation, dependencies, and container images. Git for code, Artifact Registry for Docker images, lock files for dependencies. Training script only (option B) misses preprocessing/features. Model artifacts only (option C) can't reproduce training. Environment isn't enough (option D) without code versions.
concept: ML reproducibility: Version control all code (preprocessing, features, training), dependencies (requirements.txt), containers (Docker tags), data versions, pipeline definitions. Git + Artifact Registry + data versioning.

q: You're building an ML workflow using TFX (TensorFlow Extended) on Vertex AI. Your pipeline needs to validate incoming data quality before training. Which TFX component should you use and how?
opts: Use ExampleValidator component with a schema generated by SchemaGen to detect anomalies like missing features, type mismatches, and distribution shifts
Write custom validation logic in the Trainer component
Use Evaluator component to validate data quality
Skip data validation as TFX handles it automatically
explain: TFX's ExampleValidator compares incoming data against a schema (from SchemaGen) to detect anomalies: missing features, wrong types, unexpected values, distribution shifts. This catches data quality issues before training. Custom logic in Trainer (option B) mixes concerns and runs too late. Evaluator (option C) validates model output, not input data. TFX doesn't validate automatically (option D) - you must include ExampleValidator in the pipeline.
concept: TFX data validation: SchemaGen generates schema from training data → ExampleValidator checks new data against schema → Detects anomalies before training. Catches data quality issues early.

q: Your organization uses Vertex AI Workbench for ML development. Data scientists want to share pre-configured environments (specific Python packages, system libraries) across the team. How should you implement this?
opts: Create custom container images with required dependencies, push to Artifact Registry, and use them as the base for Workbench instances
Have each data scientist manually install packages after launching instances
Share a requirements.txt file and ask everyone to run pip install
Use the default Workbench image and install packages as needed
explain: Custom container images ensure consistent environments. Build an image with all dependencies, push to Artifact Registry, configure Workbench to use this image. Everyone gets identical setup. Manual installation (option B) is error-prone and time-consuming. Requirements.txt (option C) doesn't handle system libraries and can have version conflicts. Default image (option D) doesn't include custom dependencies.
concept: Workbench environment management: Custom container image with dependencies → Artifact Registry → Configure as Workbench base. Consistent team environments. Update image, restart instances.

q: You're orchestrating a multi-step ML pipeline: data ingestion from BigQuery, preprocessing with Dataflow, training on Vertex AI, and deployment. Which orchestration tool is most appropriate?
opts: Use Vertex AI Pipelines with components for each step, as it integrates natively with all these GCP services
Use Cloud Composer (Airflow) since it's more flexible
Write custom orchestration scripts using gcloud commands
Use Cloud Scheduler to trigger each step sequentially
explain: Vertex AI Pipelines is purpose-built for ML workflows with native integration for BigQuery, Dataflow, Vertex AI Training/Endpoints. It provides artifact tracking, caching, and ML-specific features. Cloud Composer (option B) works but adds operational overhead and isn't ML-optimized. Custom scripts (option C) lack orchestration features like retry, dependency management. Scheduler (option D) can't handle dependencies and error handling properly.
concept: ML workflow orchestration on GCP: Vertex AI Pipelines for ML workflows (best GCP integration, ML features). Cloud Composer for complex cross-service workflows. Choose based on use case complexity.

q: Your TFX pipeline on Vertex AI needs to retrain the model only when data drift exceeds a threshold. The ExampleValidator component detects drift. How should you implement conditional training?
opts: Use Resolver to check ExampleValidator anomalies output, then use conditional execution in the pipeline definition to skip Trainer if no anomalies
Run ExampleValidator in a separate pipeline and manually trigger training
Configure automatic retraining in the Trainer component
Always retrain regardless of drift to ensure freshness
explain: TFX supports conditional execution: Resolver reads ExampleValidator output, pipeline logic checks for anomalies, conditionally executes Trainer. This enables drift-based retraining. Separate pipeline (option B) breaks automation. Trainer can't auto-check drift (option C). Always retraining (option D) wastes resources when data hasn't changed.
concept: TFX conditional execution: Resolver reads component outputs → Pipeline logic evaluates conditions → Conditionally execute components. Enables drift-triggered retraining, quality gates.

q: You're building a Kubeflow pipeline that includes components for training (GPU), feature engineering (CPU), and model evaluation (CPU). Your GKE cluster has both CPU and GPU node pools. How should you optimize resource usage and cost?
opts: Use node selectors or node affinity in component definitions to schedule training on GPU nodes and other components on CPU nodes
Use a GPU-only cluster since one component needs GPUs
Let Kubernetes schedule all components randomly
Use CPU-only cluster and skip GPU acceleration
explain: Node selectors/affinity assign pods to appropriate node pools: training → GPU nodes, others → CPU nodes. This optimizes cost (GPU nodes are expensive) and ensures GPU availability for training. GPU-only cluster (option B) wastes money on non-GPU workloads. Random scheduling (option C) may assign training to CPU nodes. CPU-only (option D) loses training speed.
concept: Kubeflow resource optimization: Node selectors/affinity assign pods to appropriate node pools. GPU pods → GPU nodes, CPU pods → CPU nodes. Reduces cost, ensures resource availability.

q: Your A/B test for two recommendation models needs to account for seasonality effects. Model A was tested during December (holiday season) with 8% click-through rate. Model B is being tested in January with 6% click-through rate. How should you interpret these results?
opts: The comparison is invalid due to temporal confounding; run simultaneous A/B test with random traffic split to control for seasonality
Model A is clearly better with 2% higher CTR
Normalize results by dividing each model's CTR by the average CTR for that month
Wait until December to test Model B for fair comparison
explain: Sequential testing across different time periods introduces temporal confounding - you can't separate model effect from seasonality effect. The solution is simultaneous testing: split traffic randomly between models during the same period. Both models experience identical external conditions. Declaring A better (option B) ignores confounding. Normalization (option C) assumes you know the true seasonal effect. Waiting a year (option D) is impractical.
concept: A/B test temporal validity: Avoid sequential testing across time periods (temporal confounding). Use simultaneous testing with random traffic split. Both variants experience identical external conditions.

q: You're using Vertex AI Explainable AI for a fraud detection model. Stakeholders complain that explanations are inconsistent: the same transaction explained twice shows different feature attributions. What is the most likely cause?
opts: Integrated Gradients uses sampling-based approximation; increase the number of integration steps to reduce variance and improve explanation stability
The model is non-deterministic and produces different predictions
The explanation service is misconfigured
This is expected behavior and indicates the model is working correctly
explain: Integrated Gradients approximates the integral from baseline to input using sampling. Low step count causes high variance in attributions. Increase step count (e.g., from 25 to 50-100) for more stable explanations at the cost of computation time. Model non-determinism (option B) would change predictions, not just explanations. Misconfiguration (option C) would cause errors, not variance. Inconsistent explanations (option D) indicate approximation issues, not correct behavior.
concept: Integrated Gradients stability: Sampling-based method has variance. Increase integration steps for more stable attributions. Trade-off: stability vs computation time. Typical range: 25-100 steps.
